sessions.content:
## Redis: The Swiss Army Knife of Caching

Redis is an in-memory data store that supports strings, lists, sets, sorted sets, hashes, and more.

### Why Redis Over Simple Key-Value?
```
// Simple cache: only strings
cache.set('key', 'value')

// Redis: rich data structures
redis.hset('user:123', 'name', 'Alice', 'email', 'alice@example.com')
redis.lpush('feed:user:123', postId)  // Add to list
redis.zadd('leaderboard', score, playerId)  // Sorted set
redis.sadd('online_users', userId)  // Set membership
```

### Common Redis Patterns

**1. Session Store**
```
SET session:abc123 '{"userId":1,"role":"admin"}' EX 3600
```

**2. Rate Limiter**
```
INCR rate:user:123:minute
EXPIRE rate:user:123:minute 60
// If count > 100, reject request
```

**3. Pub/Sub for Real-Time**
```
PUBLISH chat:room:42 '{"user":"Alice","msg":"Hello!"}'
SUBSCRIBE chat:room:42
```

### Redis Deployment
- **Single instance**: Simple, up to 100K ops/sec
- **Sentinel**: Automatic failover (master dies → replica promoted)
- **Cluster**: Sharded across multiple nodes for scale

### Interview Tip
'I'd use Redis for caching because it supports rich data structures beyond simple key-value, has built-in TTL, pub/sub for real-time features, and can be deployed as a cluster for horizontal scaling.'

sessions.title: CDN Caching: Edge Caching for Static Content

sessions.content:
## CDN Caching: Edge Caching for Static Content

A CDN (Content Delivery Network) caches your content at edge servers worldwide, so users get data from a server near them instead of your origin.

```mermaid
graph LR
    User_India[User India] --> Edge_India[CDN Edge India]
    User_US[User US] --> Edge_US[CDN Edge US]
    Edge_India --> |Cache Miss| Origin[Origin Server]
    Edge_US --> |Cache Miss| Origin
```

### What to Cache on CDN
- Static assets (images, CSS, JS, fonts)
- API responses that rarely change
- HTML pages (for static sites)

### Cache-Control Headers
```
Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000  // Cache for 1 year (static assets)
Cache-Control: public, max-age=60        // Cache for 1 minute (API)
Cache-Control: no-cache                   // Always revalidate with origin
Cache-Control: no-store                   // Never cache (sensitive data)
```

### CDN Providers
- **CloudFront** (AWS): Deep integration with S3, Lambda@Edge
- **Cloudflare**: Free tier, DDoS protection, Workers for edge computing
- **Akamai**: Enterprise-grade, largest network

### Interview Pattern
'For static assets, I'd use a CDN with aggressive caching (1 year TTL) and cache-busting via content hashes in filenames (e.g., `app.a1b2c3.js`). For API responses, I'd use short TTL (60s) with stale-while-revalidate.'

sessions.title: Caching Eviction Policies
sessions.paretoJustification: Redis's data structures enable advanced caching patterns beyond simple key-value

activities.description: Implement leaderboard using Redis Sorted Set (ZADD, ZRANGE, ZRANK)
Implement rate limiter using Redis with sliding window log

How does HyperLogLog trade accuracy for memory?
What is Redis SCAN and why not use KEYS?
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement 3 different use cases using appropriate Redis data structures
sessions.content:
## Caching Eviction Policies

When the cache is full, which item do you remove to make space?

### LRU (Least Recently Used)
Remove the item that hasn't been accessed for the longest time.
- **Implementation**: Doubly linked list + hash map
- **Best for**: General-purpose caching
- **Used by**: Redis (approximated LRU), Memcached

### LFU (Least Frequently Used)
Remove the item accessed the fewest times.
- **Best for**: Data with clear popularity patterns
- **Problem**: New items get evicted before they can build frequency

### FIFO (First In, First Out)
Remove the oldest item. Simple but ignores access patterns.

### Random
Remove a random item. Surprisingly effective and simple.

### Comparison
| Policy | Hit Rate | Complexity | Best For |
|--------|---------|-----------|----------|
| LRU | High | O(1) with hash+list | General purpose |
| LFU | Highest for skewed | O(log n) | Stable popularity |
| FIFO | Low | O(1) | Simple queues |
| Random | Moderate | O(1) | When simplicity > performance |

### LRU Implementation (Interview Favorite!)
```typescript
class LRUCache {
  private capacity: number;
  private map = new Map<string, any>(); // Preserves insertion order
  
  get(key: string) {
    if (!this.map.has(key)) return -1;
    const val = this.map.get(key);
    this.map.delete(key);    // Remove
    this.map.set(key, val);  // Re-insert (moves to end)
    return val;
  }
  
  put(key: string, value: any) {
    this.map.delete(key);    // Remove if exists
    this.map.set(key, value); // Insert at end
    if (this.map.size > this.capacity) {
      const firstKey = this.map.keys().next().value;
      this.map.delete(firstKey); // Remove oldest
    }
  }
}
```

sessions.title: Multi-Level Caching Architecture
sessions.paretoJustification: CDN caching is the highest-leverage caching layer for global applications

activities.description: Configure Cache-Control headers for: static assets, API responses, user-specific pages
Design content versioning strategy to avoid CDN invalidation

reviewQuestions: What is s-maxage vs max-age?
How do ETags enable conditional requests?
What is a surrogate key and how does it enable bulk CDN purging?
sessions.successCriteria: Can configure appropriate Cache-Control for any content type with justification
sessions.content:
## Multi-Level Caching Architecture

Production systems don't use just one cache — they use multiple layers.

```mermaid
graph TB
    Client[Client Browser] --> |L1| BrowserCache[Browser Cache]
    BrowserCache --> |L2| CDN[CDN Edge]
    CDN --> |L3| AppCache[App Server Cache]
    AppCache --> |L4| Redis[Redis/Memcached]
    Redis --> |L5| DB[(Database)]
```

### Layer 1: Browser Cache
HTTP cache headers tell the browser to store responses locally.
- No network request needed for cached resources
- `Cache-Control: max-age=3600`

### Layer 2: CDN
Edge servers cache content geographically close to users.
- Reduces latency by 50-90%
- Handles DDoS by absorbing traffic at the edge

### Layer 3: Application Cache
In-process cache (e.g., Node.js Map, Java ConcurrentHashMap).
- Fastest possible (no network hop)
- Limited by server RAM, lost on restart

### Layer 4: Distributed Cache (Redis/Memcached)
Shared cache across all app servers.
- Consistent view for all servers
- Survives app server restarts

### Layer 5: Database Query Cache
Database caches query results internally.
- Automatic but limited
- Invalidated on any table write

### Interview Tip
'I'd design a multi-level cache: browser cache for static assets, CDN for media and popular content, Redis for hot database queries, and in-process cache for configuration data. Each layer reduces load on the layer below it.'

sessions.title: Cache Consistency Patterns
sessions.paretoJustification: Misconfigured eviction causes silent data loss in production
objectives: Configure Redis maxmemory and eviction policies

activities.description: Configure Redis with maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru, simulate memory pressure
Implement cache hit rate monitoring using Redis INFO stats

reviewQuestions: When would you choose LFU over LRU?
What happens if Redis maxmemory is not set?
What is cache hit rate and what's a healthy target?
sessions.successCriteria: Can configure Redis memory management and monitor cache effectiveness
sessions.content:
## Cache Consistency Patterns

### The Core Problem
You have data in the database AND in the cache. When either changes, the other is stale.

### Pattern 1: Cache-Aside with TTL
Most common. Accept eventual consistency within the TTL window.
```
Write: Update DB → Delete cache entry
Read: Check cache → Miss → Read DB → Populate cache
```

### Pattern 2: Write-Through with Cache
```
Write: Update cache → Cache updates DB (synchronous)
Read: Always from cache (always fresh)
```
Guarantees consistency but adds write latency.

### Pattern 3: Event-Driven Invalidation
```
Write: Update DB → Emit event
Cache subscriber: Receive event → Invalidate/update cache
```

### The Delete vs Update Debate
**Delete from cache** (recommended):
```typescript
await db.updateUser(user);
await redis.del(`user:${user.id}`); // Next read will refetch
```

**Update the cache** (risky):
```typescript
await db.updateUser(user);
await redis.set(`user:${user.id}`, JSON.stringify(user)); // What if DB write failed?
```

**Why delete is safer**: If the DB write fails but cache update succeeds, the cache has data that doesn't exist in the database. Deleting ensures the next read fetches the truth from the database.

### Interview Answer
'I'd use cache-aside with TTL for most cases. For critical data where consistency matters, I'd use event-driven invalidation — when the database changes, a CDC (Change Data Capture) event triggers cache invalidation.'

sessions.title: Practice: Design a Caching Strategy
sessions.paretoJustification: Synthesis for interview performance
objectives: Design complete caching strategy for a system

activities.description: Mock: Design the caching layer for Twitter's home timeline (350M DAU)
Mock: Design cache invalidation for an e-commerce product catalog

reviewQuestions: Where in the stack does each cache live?
What data should NOT be cached?
How do you handle cache warming after a cache server restart?
sessions.successCriteria: Complete caching system design in 45 minutes with consistent justification
sessions.content:
## Practice: Design a Caching Strategy

### Challenge: Design the caching layer for a social media news feed

Requirements:
- 100M daily active users
- Average user follows 200 people
- Each user checks their feed 10 times/day
- Feed shows 20 posts per page

### Capacity Calculation
- 100M × 10 reads/day = 1 billion reads/day
- 1B / 86,400 seconds = ~11,500 reads/second
- Each feed page: 20 posts × ~1KB = 20KB per read

### Caching Architecture

**Layer 1: Pre-computed Feed Cache**
```
Redis: feed:{userId} → [postId1, postId2, ..., postId200]
// Store the latest 200 post IDs per user
// TTL: 5 minutes
```

**Layer 2: Post Content Cache**
```
Redis: post:{postId} → {content, author, likes, timestamp}
// Cache individual post objects
// TTL: 1 hour
```

**Layer 3: User Profile Cache**
```
Redis: user:{userId} → {name, avatar, verified}
// Cache user profiles (shown on every post)
// TTL: 24 hours
```

### Feed Generation Strategy
- **Fan-out on write**: When Alice posts, push postId to all her followers' feed caches
- **Fan-out on read**: When Bob opens his feed, pull posts from all people he follows
- **Hybrid (what Facebook/Instagram does)**: Fan-out on write for normal users, fan-out on read for celebrities (millions of followers)

### Interview Answer
'For a news feed, I'd use a three-layer caching strategy: pre-computed feed caches in Redis for the post ID lists, individual post content caches, and user profile caches. For feed generation, I'd use a hybrid approach — fan-out on write for normal users and fan-out on read for celebrities to avoid writing to millions of caches when a celebrity posts.'

quizBank.question: Which caching pattern checks the cache first, then the database on miss, then populates the cache?
quizBank.explanation: Cache-aside (lazy loading): application checks cache → cache miss → fetch from DB → write to cache → return. The application manages cache population explicitly.

quizBank.question: Which Redis data structure is best for implementing a real-time leaderboard?
quizBank.explanation: Redis Sorted Set stores members with scores, automatically sorted. ZADD adds/updates scores, ZRANK returns rank, ZRANGE returns top N — all O(log n). Perfect for leaderboards.

quizBank.question: Write-through caching always writes to both cache and database simultaneously.
quizBank.explanation: Write-through writes to cache and DB synchronously in the same write operation, ensuring cache and DB are always in sync at the cost of higher write latency.

quizBank.question: What is a cache stampede?
options: A) Cache entries being deleted too frequently
B) Many simultaneous requests hitting the DB when a popular cache key expires
D) Cache serving stale data to users
quizBank.explanation: Cache stampede (thundering herd): popular cache key expires → many concurrent requests miss cache → all hit the DB simultaneously → DB overwhelmed. Solution: distributed locking, probabilistic early expiration, or stale-while-revalidate.

quizBank.question: Explain negative caching and when to use it.
quizBank.explanation: Negative caching: storing a 'this value does not exist' marker in the cache when a DB query returns empty. Without it: every request for a non-existent key (e.g., deleted user) bypasses cache and hits DB. With it: first miss → cache 'NULL' with short TTL (e.g., 5 min) → subsequent requests return cached null without DB hit. Use when: high traffic for non-existent resources (user enumeration attacks, product 404s). Risk: cache the null too long and users see stale 'not found' after item is created.

quizBank.question: Your Redis cache has 1GB memory limit. What eviction policy would you choose for a web session store where you want to keep recent sessions?
quizBank.explanation: Choose: allkeys-lru (Least Recently Used across all keys). Session store: recent sessions are most valuable. LRU evicts sessions that haven't been accessed longest — old, likely expired sessions. Alternative: volatile-lru if only session keys have TTL and other keys should never be evicted. Avoid: noeviction (causes OOM errors when memory full). Avoid: allkeys-random (randomly evicts valuable sessions).

quizBank.question: Design a cache key naming scheme for a multi-tenant SaaS application.
quizBank.explanation: Scheme: {tenant_id}:{entity_type}:{entity_id}:{version}. Examples: tenant123:user:456 or acme:product:789:v2. Rules: (1) Always prefix with tenant_id for isolation. (2) Include entity type for readability and key-space analysis. (3) Version suffix for cache busting without deletion. (4) Avoid spaces and special chars. (5) Use consistent separator (colon is Redis convention). (6) Document the scheme in code constants, not magic strings. Benefits: easy debugging, bulk deletion by pattern (SCAN + DEL), clear ownership.

quizBank.question: How does the Cache-Control header 's-maxage' differ from 'max-age'?
quizBank.explanation: max-age: directive for ALL caches including the browser. Specifies how long content is fresh. s-maxage: directive for SHARED caches only (CDN, proxy caches). Overrides max-age for CDNs. Browser ignores s-maxage. Example: Cache-Control: max-age=60, s-maxage=3600. Browser caches for 1 minute. CDN caches for 1 hour. Use case: API responses that CDN should cache for long time but browser should always revalidate. Allows serving fresh content to each user while CDN reduces origin load.

quizBank.question: Compare LRU vs LFU eviction policies. When would LFU outperform LRU?
quizBank.explanation: LRU: evicts item not accessed in longest time. Assumes recent access = likely future access. LFU: evicts item accessed fewest times. Assumes frequency = importance. LFU outperforms LRU when: (1) Cyclical access patterns — LRU gets polluted by one-time sequential scans, LFU retains frequently accessed 'hot' data. (2) Long tail access patterns — hot items accessed millions of times are protected by frequency. (3) Product recommendation cache: product viewed 1000 times this week is more valuable than product viewed once yesterday (LRU might keep the recent one). LRU outperforms LFU when: access patterns are temporal (recent = important), workloads change suddenly (LFU is slow to adapt — requires decay function).

quizBank.question: A popular article on your news site has 100,000 concurrent readers. Its cache entry expires. How do you prevent the cache stampede?
quizBank.explanation: Multiple solutions: (1) Mutex/Distributed Lock: first requester acquires Redis lock, fetches from DB, populates cache. Others wait then use cached value. Risk: all waiters blocked. (2) Probabilistic Early Expiration (PER): as TTL decreases, probability of proactive refresh increases. One request recomputes value before expiry. No blocking. (3) Stale-while-revalidate: serve stale content immediately while background task refreshes cache asynchronously. Zero user-visible latency. (4) Jitter: randomize TTLs by ±20% to prevent synchronized expirations across many cache entries. (5) Event-based: content management system pushes cache updates proactively before TTL expires. Best for popular content: combine stale-while-revalidate + proactive push.

quizBank.question: Design the caching strategy for a social media news feed where users see posts from accounts they follow.
quizBank.explanation: Two main approaches: (1) Fan-out on write: when a post is created, push it to all followers' feed caches. Feed cache is pre-computed. Read is O(1). Write is O(followers) — problematic for celebrities with 10M followers. (2) Fan-out on read: store only posts, compute feed on read by merging posts from followed accounts. Read is expensive, write is cheap. Hybrid (Twitter/Instagram approach): Fan-out on write for normal users (< 1M followers). Fan-out on read for celebrities. Cache stores tweet IDs, not full tweet data (space efficient). Full tweet fetched from tweet cache separately. TTL: 24 hours, pre-warm cache when user opens app. Invalidation: when user unfollows, cache entry becomes stale — accept staleness, TTL handles it.

quizBank.question: True or False: Redis is always faster than Memcached for caching.
quizBank.explanation: Memcached can be faster than Redis for simple string caching because it's more memory-efficient (less overhead per key) and uses multi-threaded architecture (Redis is single-threaded per shard). For pure string cache with no need for persistence or complex data types, Memcached can outperform Redis. Redis wins for complex data types, persistence, and feature richness.

quizBank.question: What is the write-around caching pattern and when would you use it?
options: A) Write to DB, skip cache; cache populated only on reads
B) Write to cache only, sync to DB asynchronously
C) Write to both cache and DB simultaneously
D) Write to multiple cache replicas before DB
quizBank.explanation: Write-around: bypass cache on writes, write directly to DB. Cache is only populated when data is subsequently read. Use when: data is written once and rarely read (logs, audit trails, bulk imports). Prevents cache pollution with data unlikely to be re-read soon.

quizBank.question: How would you implement cache warming after a Redis server restart?
quizBank.explanation: Cache warming strategies: (1) Lazy warming: let cache fill naturally as users access data. Simple but causes high DB load and slow UX initially. (2) Proactive warming script: at startup, script fetches top N most-accessed items from DB (based on access logs/analytics) and pre-populates cache. (3) Redis persistence: use RDB snapshots or AOF - cache survives restart automatically. (4) Read-replica warming: redirect read traffic to DB read replica during warmup, gradually shift to cache as hit rate increases. (5) Staged rollout: warm cache before taking traffic (blue-green). Recommendation: Redis AOF persistence + proactive warming script for top items. Never bring cold cache into production traffic directly.

quizBank.question: Design a multi-layer cache for a global video streaming service.
quizBank.explanation: Layer 1 - Browser cache: Cache-Control: public, max-age=3600 for video metadata. Immutable for video segments. Layer 2 - CDN (Cloudflare/CloudFront): Edge caches video segments at 200+ PoPs globally. Video segments: long TTL (24h+). Metadata: shorter TTL (5 min). Surroggate keys for invalidation by show/episode. Layer 3 - Application cache (Redis): User preferences, watchlist, recommendation scores. Per-user, TTL 1 hour. Layer 4 - Database: Source of truth. Only accessed on L3 cache miss. Video storage: S3/GCS (not cached, directly from CDN). Cache invalidation: when new episode added → CDN surrogate key purge → Redis recommendation invalidation. Hot content detection: monitor CDN hit rates, pre-warm edge nodes for scheduled premieres.

quizBank.question: Explain cache coherence problems in a multi-region deployment and how to address them.
quizBank.explanation: Problem: User updates profile in US region. US Redis cache updated. EU user reads profile → EU Redis has stale data. EU cache is incoherent with truth. Solutions: (1) Write-through with cross-region replication: write to all regional caches on update. High write latency, complex. (2) Read-through with low TTL: EU cache misses often, fetches from DB. Eventual consistency. (3) Event-based invalidation: US region publishes invalidation event to global message bus. All regions delete the key. EU cache miss on next read → fresh from local DB replica. (4) Single write region: all writes go to primary region. Regional caches are read-only. Accept eventual consistency for reads. (5) Staleness SLA: define acceptable staleness (5 min). Use TTL to enforce it. The right answer depends on consistency requirements. User profiles: 5 min staleness often acceptable. Financial data: zero tolerance → don't cache or use strong consistency.

quizBank.question: What is the difference between Redis Sentinel and Redis Cluster?
quizBank.explanation: Redis Sentinel: High availability for a single Redis instance. Sentinel nodes monitor master, detect failure, promote replica to master, update client connections. Does NOT provide data sharding — all data on one master. Use for: HA without need for horizontal scale. Redis Cluster: Both HA AND horizontal sharding. Data split into 16,384 hash slots across multiple master nodes. Each master has replicas. Clients route to correct node. Use for: datasets larger than single node, horizontal write scaling. Rule of thumb: < 25GB → Sentinel. > 25GB or need write scaling → Cluster. Both provide automatic failover.

quizBank.question: How does Facebook's TAO system differ from a simple Redis cache?
quizBank.explanation: TAO (The Associations and Objects): Facebook's distributed data store for social graph. Designed specifically for graph data: objects (users, pages) and associations (friendships, likes). Key differences from Redis: (1) Purpose-built for graph: APIs for traversing edges, not just key-value. (2) Geographically distributed: regional clusters with leader region for writes, followers for reads. (3) Read-heavy optimization: 500M reads/sec. (4) Consistency model: eventual consistency for social graph (acceptable). (5) Two-tier: leaf tier (many small caches) + root tier (larger, fewer). (6) Thundering herd protection: built into TAO protocol. Redis is a general-purpose cache. TAO is application-specific, optimized for exactly Facebook's social graph access patterns.

quizBank.question: A cache has 95% hit rate. A new feature doubles the number of cacheable objects. What happens to hit rate and how do you address it?
quizBank.explanation: Hit rate likely drops: more objects compete for same cache memory → more evictions → more cache misses. Quantify: if cache held 1M items before, now needs to hold 2M but capacity unchanged. Hit rate may drop to 80-85%. Solutions: (1) Increase cache memory: scale up Redis instance or add cluster nodes. (2) Analyze access distribution: do new objects have same access frequency? If rarely accessed, they shouldn't compete with hot items. (3) Separate cache pools: hot item cache (protected, small TTL) + cold item cache (large, aggressive eviction). (4) LFU eviction: naturally deprioritizes infrequently accessed new objects. (5) Selective caching: only cache objects above access threshold. Monitor: track hit rate by key pattern to diagnose which new objects are causing evictions.

quizBank.question: What is stale-while-revalidate and how does it improve user experience?
quizBank.explanation: Stale-while-revalidate (HTTP header): Cache-Control: max-age=60, stale-while-revalidate=120. Meaning: serve content fresh for 60s. After 60s, serve stale content immediately while background task fetches fresh content. After 180s total, content is too stale — must wait for fresh fetch. User experience: user NEVER waits for cache miss. They see slightly stale content for a moment, then fresh on next request. Without it: at 60s, user waits for origin fetch (high latency). With it: user gets response in 1ms always. Trade-off: eventual consistency. Good for: news articles, product listings. Bad for: bank balances, inventory levels. In Redis: implement by serving stale + async background refresh when TTL is within threshold.

interviewTips: Always start by clarifying functional and non-functional requirements
Never jump to a solution — walk through your thought process step by step
Mention trade-offs explicitly: consistency vs availability, latency vs throughput
Draw diagrams! Use boxes for services, cylinders for databases, arrows for data flow
Start with a simple design, then scale it — don't over-engineer from the start
Always discuss failure modes: what happens when X goes down?
Mention real-world systems: 'Netflix uses X for this' shows depth
Back-of-envelope calculations impress interviewers — estimate QPS, storage, bandwidth
Discuss monitoring and observability — how would you know if something is wrong?
End with a summary of key decisions and their trade-offs
commonMistakes: Not understanding the fundamentals of Caching
Jumping to implementation without clarifying requirements
Ignoring edge cases and error handling
Not discussing trade-offs between different approaches
Memorizing solutions instead of understanding patterns

cheatSheet:
# Database Design

## SQL vs NoSQL

### SQL (Relational Databases)
- Structured data with predefined schema
- ACID transactions
- Complex queries with JOINs
- Examples: PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle, SQL Server
- Use when: financial data, complex relationships, reporting, strong consistency required

### NoSQL Categories

| Type | Examples | Use Case |
|------|----------|----------|
| Document | MongoDB, CouchDB | Flexible schemas, JSON data |
| Key-Value | Redis, DynamoDB | Sessions, caching, simple lookups |
| Wide-Column | Cassandra, HBase | Time-series, write-heavy, analytics |
| Graph | Neo4j, Amazon Neptune | Social graphs, recommendation engines |

## ACID vs BASE

### ACID (SQL)
- **Atomicity**: Transaction completes fully or not at all
- **Consistency**: DB always in valid state
- **Isolation**: Concurrent transactions don't interfere
- **Durability**: Committed data persists even after crash

### BASE (NoSQL)
- **Basically Available**: System is available most of the time
- **Soft State**: State may change without input (eventual consistency)
- **Eventually Consistent**: System converges to consistency over time

## Normalization

### 1NF: No repeating groups, atomic values
```sql
-- Bad (1NF violation)
CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INT, items VARCHAR(1000)); -- 'item1,item2,item3'

-- Good
CREATE TABLE order_items (order_id INT, item_id INT, quantity INT);
```

### 2NF: 1NF + no partial dependencies
### 3NF: 2NF + no transitive dependencies
### BCNF: Every determinant is a candidate key

## Denormalization
Intentionally violate normalization for read performance:
```sql
-- Denormalized: store username in posts to avoid JOIN
CREATE TABLE posts (
    id BIGINT PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id BIGINT,
    username VARCHAR(50),  -- denormalized from users table
    content TEXT,
    created_at TIMESTAMP
);
```
Trade-off: faster reads, harder writes, data duplication risk.

## Indexing

### B-Tree Index (Default)
- Balanced tree, O(log n) lookups
- Supports: equality, range queries, ORDER BY, GROUP BY
- Use for: most general-purpose queries

```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users(email);
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_user_date ON orders(user_id, created_at DESC);
```

### Hash Index
- O(1) lookups for exact equality
- Does NOT support range queries
- Use for: session lookups, exact ID matches

### Composite Index
- Multiple columns in one index
- Left-prefix rule: index (a, b, c) serves queries on (a), (a,b), (a,b,c) but NOT (b) or (c)

```sql
-- This index serves: WHERE user_id = ? and WHERE user_id = ? AND status = ?
-- Does NOT serve: WHERE status = ? alone
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_user_status ON orders(user_id, status, created_at);
```

### Covering Index
- Index includes all columns needed by query — no table access needed
```sql
-- Query: SELECT email, name FROM users WHERE status = 'active'
CREATE INDEX idx_covering ON users(status, email, name);  -- covers the query
```

### Partial Index
- Index only a subset of rows
```sql
-- Only index active orders (most queries only look at active)
CREATE INDEX idx_active_orders ON orders(user_id) WHERE status = 'active';
```

## Query Optimization

```sql
-- Use EXPLAIN to see query plan
EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = 123;

-- Bad: function on indexed column prevents index use
SELECT * FROM users WHERE LOWER(email) = 'user@example.com';

-- Good: store normalized data
SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = 'user@example.com';  -- store lowercase

-- Bad: SELECT *
SELECT * FROM orders;

-- Good: select only needed columns
SELECT id, user_id, total, status FROM orders;

-- Bad: N+1 query
for order in orders:
    user = db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', order.user_id)

-- Good: JOIN
SELECT orders.*, users.name FROM orders JOIN users ON orders.user_id = users.id;
```

## Connection Pooling

Database connections are expensive (100-500ms to establish). Connection pools maintain a pool of open connections.

```python
# SQLAlchemy connection pool
from sqlalchemy import create_engine

engine = create_engine(
    'postgresql://user:pass@host/db',
    pool_size=20,          # max connections in pool
    max_overflow=10,       # additional connections when pool exhausted
    pool_timeout=30,       # wait time before giving up
    pool_recycle=3600,     # recycle connections after 1 hour
    pool_pre_ping=True,    # test connection health before use
)
```

## System Design Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    App[Application] --> Pool[Connection Pool\nPgBouncer]
    Pool --> Primary[(Primary DB\nPostgreSQL)]
    Primary -->|WAL Streaming| R1[(Read Replica 1)]
    Primary -->|WAL Streaming| R2[(Read Replica 2)]
    App -->|Read Queries| R1
    App -->|Read Queries| R2
    Primary --> Backup[(S3 Backup\nDaily RDB)]
    
    subgraph Indexes
        BTree[B-Tree Index\nRange Queries]
        Hash[Hash Index\nEquality]
        GIN[GIN Index\nFull-Text Search]
    end
```

## Choosing SQL vs NoSQL

| Scenario | Choice | Reason |
|----------|--------|--------|
| Bank transactions | SQL | ACID, complex relationships |
| User sessions | Key-Value | Simple, fast, TTL support |
| Product catalog | Document | Flexible attributes per product |
| Social graph | Graph | Relationship traversal |
| IoT sensor data | Wide-column | High write throughput, time-series |
| Full-text search | Elasticsearch | Inverted index, relevance scoring |

## Common FAANG Interview Questions
1. Design the database schema for Twitter/Instagram/Uber
2. How do you optimize a slow SQL query?
3. When would you choose MongoDB over PostgreSQL?
4. What is an N+1 query problem and how do you fix it?
5. How do you design a schema for a multi-tenant SaaS application?

plan.overview: Database design is about modeling reality correctly, then optimizing for access patterns. The mental model: (1) Model the data correctly first (normalization). (2) Optimize for your specific read/write patterns (indexes, denormalization). (3) Choose the right database type for the data model. Never pre-optimize before you understand access patterns.
plan.skippedTopics: We skip database replication/sharding (covered in Database Scaling topic). We skip specific stored procedure/trigger syntax (implementation-specific). We skip data warehousing (covered in Data Storage topic).

sessions.content:
## Why Database Design Matters

Every application you'll ever build stores data. The difference between a system that handles 100 users and one that handles 100 million users often comes down to how you designed your database.

**Bad database design** leads to:
- Slow queries (seconds instead of milliseconds)
- Data inconsistency (user sees stale data)
- Scaling nightmares (can't add more capacity)

### The Relational Model
Most databases use the **relational model** invented by Edgar Codd in 1970. Data is organized into **tables** (relations), each with **rows** (records) and **columns** (attributes).

```sql
CREATE TABLE users (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  email VARCHAR(255) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

### Interview Tip
When asked about database design, always start with: 'Let me first understand the read/write patterns and the relationships between entities.' This shows you think before you design.

sessions.title: Normalization: Eliminating Redundancy
sessions.paretoJustification: Indexes are the single most impactful optimization in SQL databases

activities.description: Read 'Use the Index, Luke' chapters 1-3, draw B-tree diagram
Design indexes for 5 given queries, explain left-prefix rule

reviewQuestions: Why is index on LOWER(email) an anti-pattern?
What is an index-only scan?
How does a composite index differ from two separate indexes?
sessions.successCriteria: Can design optimal indexes for any given set of queries
sessions.content:
## Normalization: Eliminating Redundancy

Normalization is the process of organizing data to reduce redundancy and improve data integrity.

### First Normal Form (1NF)
Every column contains atomic (indivisible) values. No arrays or nested structures.

```
-- BAD: Not 1NF
| name  | phone_numbers        |
|-------|---------------------|
| Alice | 555-1234, 555-5678  |

-- GOOD: 1NF
| name  | phone_number |
|-------|-------------|
| Alice | 555-1234     |
| Alice | 555-5678     |
```

### Second Normal Form (2NF)
Must be in 1NF + every non-key column depends on the ENTIRE primary key (not just part of it).

### Third Normal Form (3NF)
Must be in 2NF + no transitive dependencies (column A depends on column B which depends on the key).

### When to Stop Normalizing
- **OLTP systems** (transactions): Normalize to 3NF — data integrity matters
- **OLAP systems** (analytics): Denormalize — query speed matters
- **Interview answer**: 'I'd normalize to 3NF for write-heavy systems and denormalize for read-heavy analytics workloads.'

sessions.title: Denormalization: Trading Integrity for Speed
sessions.paretoJustification: Slow queries are the most common production database problem
objectives: Read and interpret EXPLAIN ANALYZE output

activities.description: Take 5 slow queries, run EXPLAIN ANALYZE, identify issues (sequential scans, hash joins, N+1)
Fix each query using indexes and query restructuring, verify improvement

reviewQuestions: What does a Seq Scan in EXPLAIN output tell you?
When is a hash join preferred over nested loop join?
How do you fix an N+1 query in an ORM?
sessions.successCriteria: Can diagnose and fix slow queries using EXPLAIN ANALYZE
sessions.content:
## Denormalization: Trading Integrity for Speed

Sometimes normalized data is too slow to query. Denormalization intentionally adds redundancy to speed up reads.

### Example: Social Media Feed
```sql
-- Normalized (slow): 3 JOINs to show a post
SELECT p.content, u.name, u.avatar, COUNT(l.id) as likes
FROM posts p
JOIN users u ON p.user_id = u.id
LEFT JOIN likes l ON l.post_id = p.id
GROUP BY p.id, u.name, u.avatar;

-- Denormalized (fast): everything in one table
SELECT content, author_name, author_avatar, like_count
FROM posts_denormalized;
```

### Trade-offs
| | Normalized | Denormalized |
|---|---|---|
| Writes | Fast (update one place) | Slow (update many places) |
| Reads | Slow (many JOINs) | Fast (single table scan) |
| Storage | Efficient | Wasteful |
| Consistency | Always consistent | May be temporarily inconsistent |

### Real-World: Instagram
Instagram stores a denormalized copy of user profiles alongside every post. When you see a post, it doesn't JOIN to the users table — the author's name and avatar are pre-copied. When a user changes their avatar, a background job updates all their posts.

sessions.content:
## Indexing: The Key to Fast Queries

Without an index, the database scans EVERY row to find your data. With an index, it jumps directly to the right row.

### B-Tree Index (Default)
Most databases use B-tree indexes. Think of it like a phone book — sorted alphabetically, you can quickly flip to 'Smith' without reading every name.

```sql
-- Create an index on email
CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users(email);

-- This query now uses the index (fast!)
SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = 'alice@example.com';
-- Without index: O(n) full table scan
-- With index: O(log n) B-tree lookup
```

### Composite Index
```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_user_date ON orders(user_id, created_at);
-- Fast: WHERE user_id = 5 AND created_at > '2024-01-01'
-- Fast: WHERE user_id = 5 (leftmost prefix)
-- SLOW: WHERE created_at > '2024-01-01' (can't use index)
```

**Rule**: The leftmost prefix of a composite index can be used independently. Order matters!

### When NOT to Index
- Small tables (full scan is faster than index lookup)
- Columns with low cardinality (boolean columns)
- Write-heavy tables (indexes slow down writes)

### EXPLAIN — Your Best Friend
```sql
EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = 'alice@example.com';
-- Shows: Index Scan vs Seq Scan, actual time, rows scanned
```

sessions.title: ACID Properties and Transactions

sessions.content:
## ACID Properties and Transactions

ACID is the gold standard for database reliability. Every transaction guarantees:

**A — Atomicity**: All operations succeed or all fail. No partial updates.
```sql
BEGIN TRANSACTION;
  UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 1; -- Debit
  UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 100 WHERE id = 2; -- Credit
COMMIT; -- Both happen, or neither happens
```

**C — Consistency**: Database moves from one valid state to another.

**I — Isolation**: Concurrent transactions don't interfere with each other.

**D — Durability**: Once committed, data survives crashes (written to disk).

### Isolation Levels
| Level | Dirty Read | Non-Repeatable Read | Phantom Read |
|-------|-----------|--------------------|--------------|
| Read Uncommitted | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Read Committed | No | Yes | Yes |
| Repeatable Read | No | No | Yes |
| Serializable | No | No | No |

### Interview Answer
'Most production systems use Read Committed (PostgreSQL default) or Repeatable Read (MySQL InnoDB default). Serializable is safest but slowest. I'd only use it for financial transactions where correctness is critical.'

sessions.title: SQL vs NoSQL: Choosing the Right Database
sessions.paretoJustification: Connection management is often the hidden bottleneck in production

Load test with and without connection pooling, measure throughput

How do you size a connection pool?

sessions.content:
## SQL vs NoSQL: Choosing the Right Database

This is one of the most common interview questions. The answer is NEVER 'SQL is better' or 'NoSQL is better' — it depends on your use case.

### SQL Databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL)
- **Best for**: Structured data with relationships, transactions, complex queries
- **Examples**: User accounts, orders, financial records
- **Schema**: Fixed (must define columns upfront)

### NoSQL Types

**Document Store (MongoDB)**: JSON-like documents, flexible schema
```json
{
  "user_id": "123",
  "name": "Alice",
  "addresses": [{"city": "Mumbai"}, {"city": "Delhi"}]
}
```
**Best for**: Content management, user profiles, catalogs

**Key-Value Store (Redis)**: Simple key → value pairs, blazing fast
```
SET user:123:name "Alice"
GET user:123:name → "Alice"
```
**Best for**: Caching, sessions, rate limiting

**Column-Family Store (Cassandra)**: Wide columns, massive scale
**Best for**: Time-series data, IoT, write-heavy workloads

**Graph Database (Neo4j)**: Nodes and relationships
**Best for**: Social networks, recommendation engines

### Decision Framework
```
Need transactions? → SQL
Need flexible schema? → Document DB
Need blazing fast reads? → Key-Value
Need massive write throughput? → Column-Family
Need relationship traversal? → Graph DB
```

sessions.paretoJustification: SaaS database design is a common senior-level interview topic

Implement PostgreSQL row-level security for shared table approach

sessions.content:
## Data Modeling for Real Systems

Let's design actual schemas for common interview problems.

### E-Commerce Schema
```sql
CREATE TABLE products (
  id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  name VARCHAR(255),
  price DECIMAL(10,2),
  category_id INT REFERENCES categories(id),
  inventory_count INT DEFAULT 0
);

CREATE TABLE orders (
  id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  user_id BIGINT REFERENCES users(id),
  total DECIMAL(10,2),
  status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'pending',
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);

CREATE TABLE order_items (
  order_id BIGINT REFERENCES orders(id),
  product_id BIGINT REFERENCES products(id),
  quantity INT,
  price DECIMAL(10,2), -- Price at time of purchase!
  PRIMARY KEY (order_id, product_id)
);
```

### Key Design Decisions
1. **Price in order_items**: Store the price at purchase time, not a reference to the current price
2. **Soft deletes**: Use `deleted_at` instead of actually deleting
3. **Status as enum**: `pending → confirmed → shipped → delivered`

### Interview Tip
Always ask: 'What are the most common queries?' Design your schema around the read patterns, not the write patterns.

sessions.title: Connection Pooling and Performance
sessions.paretoJustification: Zero-downtime migrations are essential in production systems

activities.description: Add a NOT NULL column to a million-row table without downtime — implement expand/contract pattern
Use pt-online-schema-change or pg_repack for large table migration

reviewQuestions: Why does adding a NOT NULL column lock a large table?
What is the expand/contract migration pattern?
How do you handle column renames in a running system?
sessions.successCriteria: Can design zero-downtime migration strategy for any schema change
sessions.content:
## Connection Pooling and Performance

Creating a new database connection takes 20-50ms. If every request creates a new connection, you waste enormous time.

### Connection Pool Pattern
```typescript
import { Pool } from 'pg';

const pool = new Pool({
  host: 'localhost',
  database: 'myapp',
  max: 20,          // Max connections in pool
  idleTimeoutMillis: 30000,
  connectionTimeoutMillis: 2000,
});

// Reuse connections from the pool
const result = await pool.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1', [userId]);
```

### How It Works
```mermaid
graph LR
    R1[Request 1] --> Pool[Connection Pool]
    R2[Request 2] --> Pool
    R3[Request 3] --> Pool
    Pool --> C1[Conn 1]
    Pool --> C2[Conn 2]
    Pool --> C3[Conn 3]
    C1 --> DB[(Database)]
    C2 --> DB
    C3 --> DB
```

### Sizing the Pool
- Too few connections: Requests queue up, latency increases
- Too many connections: Database overwhelmed, context switching overhead
- **Rule of thumb**: `pool_size = (CPU cores × 2) + disk_spindles`
- For SSD: `pool_size = CPU cores × 4` (typically 20-50 for web apps)

### Common Mistake
Opening a new connection per request in serverless functions (Lambda). Solution: Use connection pooling services like PgBouncer or RDS Proxy.

reviewQuestions: What index does this query need?
How do you handle the balance update race condition?
What would you change if writes grew 10x?
sessions.successCriteria: Complete database design with schema, indexes, and scaling considerations in 45 minutes
sessions.content:
## Practice: Design a Database for a Real System

### Challenge: Design the database for a ride-sharing app (like Uber)

Requirements:
- Users can be drivers or riders
- Riders request rides, drivers accept
- Track ride history, ratings, payments
- Need to find nearby available drivers FAST

### Step 1: Identify Entities
- Users (drivers and riders)
- Rides
- Payments
- Ratings
- Driver locations (real-time)

### Step 2: Schema Design
```sql
CREATE TABLE users (
  id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  name VARCHAR(100),
  email VARCHAR(255) UNIQUE,
  role VARCHAR(10) CHECK (role IN ('rider', 'driver')),
  rating DECIMAL(2,1) DEFAULT 5.0
);

CREATE TABLE rides (
  id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  rider_id BIGINT REFERENCES users(id),
  driver_id BIGINT REFERENCES users(id),
  pickup_lat DECIMAL(10,8),
  pickup_lng DECIMAL(11,8),
  dropoff_lat DECIMAL(10,8),
  dropoff_lng DECIMAL(11,8),
  status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'requested',
  fare DECIMAL(10,2),
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

### Step 3: The Hard Part — Geospatial Queries
Finding nearby drivers requires a geospatial index:
```sql
-- PostGIS extension for PostgreSQL
CREATE INDEX idx_driver_location ON driver_locations USING GIST(location);

-- Find drivers within 5km
SELECT * FROM driver_locations
WHERE ST_DWithin(location, ST_MakePoint(72.8777, 19.0760), 5000)
AND is_available = true;
```

### Interview Talking Points
- SQL for rides/users/payments (need ACID transactions for payments)
- Redis for real-time driver locations (fast reads/writes)
- PostGIS for geospatial queries
- Separate read/write databases for scale

quizBank.question: What does ACID stand for?
quizBank.explanation: ACID: Atomicity (all-or-nothing transactions), Consistency (DB always valid), Isolation (concurrent transactions don't interfere), Durability (committed data persists after crash).

quizBank.question: Which type of index is best for a query with a range condition like WHERE age BETWEEN 20 AND 30?
quizBank.explanation: B-Tree indexes support range queries because they maintain sorted order. Hash indexes only support exact equality lookups and cannot serve range conditions.

quizBank.question: The left-prefix rule for composite indexes means a composite index on (a, b, c) can serve a query filtering only on column b.
quizBank.explanation: Left-prefix rule: composite index (a, b, c) can serve queries filtering on (a), (a,b), or (a,b,c) but NOT queries filtering only on b or c. The query must use the leftmost columns of the index.

quizBank.question: What is denormalization and when is it appropriate?
quizBank.explanation: Denormalization: intentionally introducing redundancy by storing derived or duplicated data to improve read performance. Example: storing username in posts table to avoid JOIN with users table. Appropriate when: (1) Read performance is critical and JOIN is too slow, (2) Data changes infrequently, (3) Write performance can be sacrificed, (4) Analytics workloads where joins across huge tables are prohibitive. Risk: data inconsistency (denormalized copy gets out of sync with source of truth). Mitigate with triggers or application-level consistency.

quizBank.question: Design a database schema for a Twitter-like system. What tables and indexes would you create?
quizBank.explanation: Core tables: users(id, username, email, created_at), tweets(id, user_id, content, created_at, reply_to_id), follows(follower_id, followee_id, created_at), likes(user_id, tweet_id, created_at), hashtags(id, tag), tweet_hashtags(tweet_id, hashtag_id). Key indexes: tweets(user_id, created_at DESC) for user timeline. follows(follower_id) for 'who do I follow'. follows(followee_id) for 'who follows me'. likes(tweet_id) for like count. likes(user_id, tweet_id) unique for dedup. Consider: denormalize like_count on tweets for fast display. Partition tweets by created_at for archival.

quizBank.question: Explain the N+1 query problem and how to fix it.
quizBank.explanation: N+1 problem: 1 query fetches N records, then N separate queries fetch related data for each record. Example: fetch 100 orders (1 query), then for each order fetch user details (100 queries) = 101 total queries. Fix: (1) JOIN: SELECT orders.*, users.name FROM orders JOIN users ON orders.user_id = users.id. (2) Eager loading in ORM: Order.includes(:user) in Rails. (3) DataLoader pattern: batch N user IDs into one SELECT WHERE id IN (?). (4) Denormalization: store username in orders table. Detection: count queries in logs, look for repeated similar queries.

quizBank.question: A query using WHERE email = ? is slow despite an index on email. What could cause this?
quizBank.explanation: Common causes: (1) Function on column: WHERE LOWER(email) = ... prevents index use. Store email as lowercase in DB. (2) Implicit type conversion: email column is VARCHAR but query passes integer. (3) Index not being used due to outdated statistics: run ANALYZE. (4) Low selectivity: email column has few distinct values, query planner chooses seq scan. (5) Leading wildcard: WHERE email LIKE '%@gmail.com' - index cannot serve leading wildcard. (6) Wrong collation: case-insensitive comparison bypasses index. Diagnose: EXPLAIN ANALYZE to confirm index is being used.

quizBank.question: What is the difference between REPEATABLE READ and SERIALIZABLE isolation levels?
quizBank.explanation: REPEATABLE READ: prevents dirty reads and non-repeatable reads. Guarantees that if you read a row twice in a transaction, you get the same value. Does NOT prevent phantom reads — a new row inserted by another transaction may appear in a subsequent range query. SERIALIZABLE: highest isolation level. Prevents all anomalies including phantom reads. Serializes transactions as if executed one-by-one (even though they run concurrently). Higher performance cost due to predicate locking or MVCC checks. Use SERIALIZABLE for: financial transfers, inventory management. Use REPEATABLE READ for: reports that need consistent view of data mid-transaction.

quizBank.question: You need to add a NOT NULL column to a 500-million-row table in production. How do you do it without downtime?
quizBank.explanation: Expand/Contract pattern: Phase 1 (Expand): Add column as nullable: ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN new_status VARCHAR(20). Deploy code that writes BOTH old and new column. Phase 2 (Backfill): Batch update: UPDATE orders SET new_status = old_status WHERE new_status IS NULL LIMIT 1000. Run in batches to avoid locking. Phase 3 (Enforce): Once backfill complete, add NOT NULL constraint: ALTER TABLE orders ALTER COLUMN new_status SET NOT NULL. In PostgreSQL, adding NOT NULL with a DEFAULT does a metadata-only change (no table rewrite) since PostgreSQL 11. Phase 4 (Contract): Remove old column in a future deployment.

quizBank.question: When would you choose Cassandra over PostgreSQL?
quizBank.explanation: Choose Cassandra when: (1) Write-heavy workload: Cassandra's LSM-tree is optimized for high write throughput. (2) Time-series data: events, metrics, logs appended by timestamp. (3) Linear horizontal scalability: add nodes and capacity grows linearly. No master-slave. (4) Geographic distribution: multi-region active-active writes. (5) Known access patterns: Cassandra requires designing tables for specific query patterns. (6) Availability over consistency: Cassandra is AP in CAP. Choose PostgreSQL when: (1) Complex relational queries with JOINs. (2) ACID transactions required. (3) Schema is flexible and evolving. (4) Reporting and ad-hoc queries. (5) Consistency is critical (financial data).

quizBank.question: Design a database schema for a multi-tenant SaaS CRM application.
quizBank.explanation: Three approaches: (1) Shared database, shared schema: all tenants in same tables with tenant_id column. Add Row Level Security: CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON contacts USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.tenant_id')::uuid). Pros: operationally simple, efficient resource use. Cons: noisy neighbor, complex RLS. (2) Shared database, separate schemas: each tenant gets own schema. Pros: clean isolation, easy per-tenant backup. Cons: schema migrations run N times. (3) Database per tenant: complete isolation. Pros: ultimate isolation. Cons: expensive, hard to manage 10k databases. Recommendation for < 10k tenants: shared schema with RLS. For compliance/enterprise: separate schema or database.

quizBank.question: What is an index-only scan and when does it occur?
quizBank.explanation: Index-only scan: query fetches all required data from the index without accessing the main table (heap). Occurs when: SELECT only includes columns that are all part of the index (covering index). Example: CREATE INDEX idx ON users(status, email, name); SELECT email, name FROM users WHERE status = 'active' — all 3 columns in query exist in index, no heap access needed. Extremely fast because: (1) Index is smaller than table (fewer I/O), (2) Index is typically in memory, (3) No random access to heap. Create covering indexes for your most critical queries.

quizBank.question: True or False: A foreign key constraint automatically creates an index on the referencing column.
quizBank.explanation: PostgreSQL and MySQL InnoDB do NOT automatically create indexes on foreign key columns. You must create the index manually: CREATE INDEX ON orders(user_id). Without the index, DELETE/UPDATE on the parent table requires a sequential scan of the child table to check for referencing rows — very slow at scale.

quizBank.question: What is optimistic locking and when does it fail?
quizBank.explanation: Optimistic locking: assume no conflict, check on write. Add version column to table. Read: SELECT *, version FROM products WHERE id = 1. Modify in application. Write: UPDATE products SET quantity = ?, version = version+1 WHERE id = 1 AND version = {read_version}. If 0 rows updated → conflict detected, retry. Fails when: high contention (many concurrent writes to same row) causes many retries. Example: flash sale — thousands of concurrent requests updating same product inventory all fail and retry, causing thundering herd. Solution for high contention: pessimistic locking (SELECT FOR UPDATE) or queue-based serialization.

quizBank.question: How do you handle storing hierarchical/tree data in a relational database?
quizBank.explanation: Four patterns: (1) Adjacency List: parent_id column. Simple but recursive queries are slow (N+1 for N levels). Use WITH RECURSIVE CTE in PostgreSQL. (2) Path Enumeration: store full path '/root/child/grandchild'. Simple queries. Problem: path updates cascade. (3) Nested Sets: left/right boundaries. Fast read for subtrees. Complex writes. (4) Closure Table: separate table storing all ancestor-descendant pairs. Fast reads and writes, uses more storage. Best for deep trees with frequent reads: Closure Table or Nested Sets. Best for simple tree with moderate depth: Adjacency List with RECURSIVE CTE. PostgreSQL ltree extension for hierarchical data.

quizBank.question: Design an audit trail system that records all changes to a users table.
quizBank.explanation: Options: (1) Application-level: before each UPDATE/DELETE, INSERT into users_audit table. users_audit(id, user_id, operation, old_value JSONB, new_value JSONB, changed_by, changed_at). (2) Database trigger: CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION audit_user_changes() RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$ BEGIN INSERT INTO users_audit VALUES (NEW.id, TG_OP, row_to_json(OLD), row_to_json(NEW), current_user, NOW()); RETURN NEW; END; $$ LANGUAGE plpgsql; CREATE TRIGGER user_audit_trigger AFTER UPDATE OR DELETE ON users FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION audit_user_changes(); (3) Temporal tables: PostgreSQL doesn't have built-in temporal tables but temporal_tables extension adds them. (4) CDC (Change Data Capture): Debezium captures WAL changes. Recommendation: triggers for simplicity, CDC for at-scale with Kafka pipeline.

quizBank.question: What is connection pooling and how do you size a connection pool?
quizBank.explanation: Connection pooling: maintain N open DB connections, reuse them across requests. Without pooling: each request creates/destroys connection (100-500ms overhead). With pooling: connection reuse, acquire in <1ms. Sizing formula: connections = ((core_count * 2) + effective_spindle_count). For SSD: effective_spindle_count = 1. 8-core server with SSD: 8*2+1 = 17 connections optimal. PgBouncer pool_size: start with 20, monitor pg_stat_activity. Signs of undersizing: clients waiting in queue. Signs of oversizing: DB memory exhaustion, context switching overhead. Rule of thumb: PostgreSQL handles 100-200 connections, use PgBouncer to multiplex thousands of app connections into this pool.

quizBank.question: Explain the CAP theorem as it relates to database selection.
quizBank.explanation: CAP theorem: in a distributed system, you can only guarantee 2 of 3: Consistency (every read gets most recent write), Availability (every request gets a response), Partition tolerance (system works despite network failures). Since network failures (partitions) are inevitable, choose CA or CP: CA systems: sacrifice availability during partition (single-node databases: PostgreSQL, MySQL). CP systems: sacrifice availability to maintain consistency during partition (HBase, Zookeeper, MongoDB in default mode). AP systems: sacrifice consistency for availability (Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB). Most NoSQL databases are AP. Most RDBMS are CP. Choose based on: can your system tolerate stale reads? (AP) vs. does stale data cause correctness bugs? (CP)

quizBank.question: What is a covering index and how does it improve query performance?
options: A) An index that covers all tables in the database
B) An index containing all columns needed by a query, eliminating table access
C) An index that automatically updates when data changes
D) An index that spans multiple databases
quizBank.explanation: A covering index includes all columns referenced in a query (SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY), allowing the database to answer the query entirely from the index without accessing the main table (heap). This dramatically improves performance by avoiding random I/O to the table.

quizBank.question: How would you model a many-to-many relationship between Products and Tags?
quizBank.explanation: Create a junction table: products(id, name, price), tags(id, name), product_tags(product_id, tag_id, created_at). Primary key on (product_id, tag_id). Add index on (tag_id, product_id) for querying products by tag. Queries: -- Products with all their tags: SELECT p.*, array_agg(t.name) as tags FROM products p JOIN product_tags pt ON p.id = pt.product_id JOIN tags t ON t.id = pt.tag_id GROUP BY p.id; -- Products with a specific tag: SELECT p.* FROM products p JOIN product_tags pt ON p.id = pt.product_id JOIN tags t ON t.id = pt.tag_id WHERE t.name = 'electronics';

quizBank.question: A PostgreSQL table has 10 million rows. A query SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status = 'pending' runs a sequential scan despite an index on status. Why?
quizBank.explanation: Query planner chose seq scan over index scan because: 'pending' status likely has high cardinality in the data (e.g., 80% of 10M rows are 'pending'). For high-selectivity queries (returning large % of table), sequential scan is FASTER than index scan because: (1) Index scan = random I/O, touching each row's page separately. (2) Sequential scan = sequential I/O, reading contiguous pages efficiently. The optimizer estimates rows returned and if > ~5-10% of table, prefers seq scan. Solutions: (1) Partial index: CREATE INDEX ON orders(created_at) WHERE status = 'pending'. Low cardinality subset is now small and indexed. (2) Reconsider query: filter by more selective column (user_id + status). (3) Accept seq scan: if table fits in RAM, it may be fast enough.

interviewTips: Always start by clarifying functional and non-functional requirements
Never jump to a solution — walk through your thought process step by step
Mention trade-offs explicitly: consistency vs availability, latency vs throughput
Draw diagrams! Use boxes for services, cylinders for databases, arrows for data flow
Start with a simple design, then scale it — don't over-engineer from the start
Always discuss failure modes: what happens when X goes down?
Mention real-world systems: 'Netflix uses X for this' shows depth
Back-of-envelope calculations impress interviewers — estimate QPS, storage, bandwidth
Discuss monitoring and observability — how would you know if something is wrong?
End with a summary of key decisions and their trade-offs
commonMistakes: Not understanding the fundamentals of Database Design
Jumping to implementation without clarifying requirements
Ignoring edge cases and error handling
Not discussing trade-offs between different approaches
Memorizing solutions instead of understanding patterns

cheatSheet:
# Database Scaling

## The Scaling Problem
A single database server has limits: CPU, RAM, disk I/O, network bandwidth. As data and traffic grow, you need strategies to scale beyond single-node limits.

## Read Replicas
Most applications are read-heavy (80-95% reads). Offload reads to replicas:

```
Writes → Primary DB
Reads  → Read Replica 1, 2, 3
```

```python
# Route reads to replica, writes to primary
class DatabaseRouter:
    def db_for_read(self, model):
        return 'replica'
    
    def db_for_write(self, model):
        return 'primary'
```

**Replication lag**: replica may be seconds behind primary. For reads requiring latest data (after a write), read from primary or wait.

## Replication Types

### Master-Slave (Primary-Replica)
- One primary handles all writes
- Replicas stream changes from primary
- Replicas can handle reads
- Failover: promote a replica to primary

```mermaid
graph LR
    App[Application] -->|Writes| P[(Primary)]
    App -->|Reads| R1[(Replica 1)]
    App -->|Reads| R2[(Replica 2)]
    P -->|WAL Streaming| R1
    P -->|WAL Streaming| R2
```

### Master-Master (Multi-Primary)
- Multiple nodes accept writes
- All sync changes with each other
- Conflict resolution needed (last-write-wins, custom)
- Use case: geographic distribution (each region has its own primary)
- Risk: write conflicts, split-brain

### Synchronous vs Asynchronous Replication
- **Sync**: primary waits for replica to confirm. No data loss, higher latency.
- **Async**: primary proceeds without waiting. Lower latency, risk of data loss on failover.

## Sharding (Horizontal Partitioning)

Split data across multiple database servers. Each server holds a subset of data.

### Hash Sharding
```python
def get_shard(user_id: int, num_shards: int) -> int:
    return user_id % num_shards
```
- Even distribution
- Problem: resharding requires moving data when num_shards changes

### Range Sharding
```
Shard 1: user_id 0-999,999
Shard 2: user_id 1,000,000-1,999,999
Shard 3: user_id 2,000,000+
```
- Supports range queries within a shard
- Risk: hotspot if one range is more active

### Geographic Sharding
```
Shard US: Users in North America
Shard EU: Users in Europe (also for GDPR compliance)
Shard APAC: Users in Asia-Pacific
```
- Reduces latency (data near users)
- Enables data residency compliance

### Directory-Based Sharding
- Central lookup service maps entity → shard
- Most flexible (can move data between shards)
- Single point of failure (if lookup service is down)

## Consistent Hashing

Solves the resharding problem:
- Servers arranged on a ring (0 to 2^32)
- Data mapped to ring position
- Stored on next clockwise server
- Adding/removing server: only adjacent data moves

```python
import hashlib
from bisect import bisect

class ConsistentHashRing:
    def __init__(self, nodes, virtual_nodes=150):
        self.ring = {}
        self.sorted_keys = []
        for node in nodes:
            for i in range(virtual_nodes):
                key = self._hash(f'{node}:{i}')
                self.ring[key] = node
                self.sorted_keys.append(key)
        self.sorted_keys.sort()
    
    def _hash(self, key):
        return int(hashlib.md5(key.encode()).hexdigest(), 16)
    
    def get_node(self, key):
        h = self._hash(key)
        idx = bisect(self.sorted_keys, h) % len(self.sorted_keys)
        return self.ring[self.sorted_keys[idx]]
```

## Vertical Partitioning

Split table columns into separate tables:
```sql
-- Before: one wide users table
users(id, name, email, password_hash, bio, avatar_url, settings_json, ...)

-- After: split infrequently accessed columns
users(id, name, email)           -- frequently accessed
user_profiles(user_id, bio, avatar_url)  -- less frequent
user_settings(user_id, settings_json)    -- rarely accessed
```

## Table Partitioning (Within a Database)

Logically split one table into multiple physical partitions:

```sql
-- PostgreSQL: range partitioning by date
CREATE TABLE events (
    id BIGINT,
    user_id BIGINT,
    event_type VARCHAR(50),
    created_at TIMESTAMP
) PARTITION BY RANGE (created_at);

CREATE TABLE events_2024_01 PARTITION OF events
    FOR VALUES FROM ('2024-01-01') TO ('2024-02-01');

CREATE TABLE events_2024_02 PARTITION OF events
    FOR VALUES FROM ('2024-02-01') TO ('2024-03-01');
-- Old partitions can be dropped in O(1) — no DELETE overhead
```

## Scaling Strategies Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Problem[DB Performance Problem] --> ReadHeavy{Read Heavy?}
    ReadHeavy -->|Yes| AddReplica[Add Read Replicas]
    ReadHeavy -->|No| WriteHeavy{Write Heavy?}
    WriteHeavy -->|Yes| Shard[Implement Sharding]
    WriteHeavy -->|No| DataSize{Data Size Large?}
    DataSize -->|Yes| Partition[Table Partitioning + Archival]
    DataSize -->|No| Cache[Add Caching Layer]
    AddReplica --> Cache
    Shard --> ConsistentHash[Consistent Hashing]
```

## CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)

Separate read and write models:
- Write side: normalized, optimized for writes, ACID
- Read side: denormalized read models, optimized for specific queries
- Sync via events or replication

## Common Sharding Challenges

1. **Cross-shard JOINs**: impossible directly → application-level join or denormalization
2. **Distributed transactions**: complex → use saga pattern or accept eventual consistency  
3. **Global secondary indexes**: expensive → scatter-gather queries or maintain separate index
4. **Rebalancing**: when shard sizes diverge → consistent hashing minimizes impact

## Real-World Examples
- **Instagram**: sharded PostgreSQL by user_id using consistent hashing
- **Uber**: sharded MySQL by city/region for geographic locality
- **WhatsApp**: sharded Mnesia (Erlang DB) by user_id
- **Pinterest**: range-sharded MySQL with consistent hashing overlay

## Common FAANG Interview Questions
1. How would you scale a database to handle 1M writes/second?
2. What is consistent hashing and why is it better than modulo hashing for sharding?
3. How do you handle cross-shard transactions?
4. How do you rebalance shards without downtime?
5. When would you choose sharding vs adding read replicas?

plan.overview: Database scaling is a spectrum: start simple (read replicas), add complexity only as needed (sharding). The golden rule: sharding is a last resort because of the massive complexity it adds. Most systems can run on a well-tuned single primary with read replicas for years.
plan.skippedTopics: We skip specific cloud DB products (RDS, Aurora, Spanner) in depth — covered in cloud-specific resources. We skip database migration tools (Flyway, Liquibase) — separate concern. We focus on architecture patterns applicable to any database.

sessions.title: Why Single Databases Fail at Scale
sessions.paretoJustification: Understanding when to apply each strategy prevents over-engineering
objectives: Map scaling strategies to traffic levels
Identify current bottleneck before choosing solution

Draw decision tree: given these symptoms, which scaling strategy applies?

reviewQuestions: What is the first scaling step when reads are slow?
What symptoms indicate you need sharding?
Why is vertical scaling considered first?
sessions.successCriteria: Can prescribe the right scaling strategy given a set of database symptoms
sessions.content:
## Why Single Databases Fail at Scale

Your startup is growing. You started with one PostgreSQL database handling everything. Then this happens:

- **100 users**: Everything is fast. Life is good.
- **10,000 users**: Queries start slowing down. You add indexes.
- **100,000 users**: Writes are bottlenecked. One write per ms = 1000 writes/sec max.
- **1,000,000 users**: Database is the bottleneck. Reads are slow, writes are queued.

### The Three Walls
1. **CPU wall**: Complex queries eat CPU. More users = more queries.
2. **Memory wall**: Hot data doesn't fit in RAM anymore. Disk reads are 100x slower.
3. **Storage wall**: Disk is full. Can't store more data on one machine.

### The Solution Spectrum
```
Single DB → Read Replicas → Sharding → Distributed DB
(simple)                                    (complex)
```

Always start simple and scale only when you have a measured problem. Premature optimization is the root of all evil.

sessions.title: Read Replicas: Scaling Reads
sessions.paretoJustification: Read replicas solve 80% of DB scaling problems with minimal complexity

activities.description: Set up PostgreSQL primary + 2 replicas using streaming replication
Implement read-your-own-writes: after a write, route next read to primary for 1s

reviewQuestions: How do you ensure a user sees their own writes despite replication lag?
What is the maximum acceptable replication lag?
What happens to replicas if primary fails?
sessions.successCriteria: Working primary-replica setup with application-level read routing
sessions.content:
## Read Replicas: Scaling Reads

Most applications are read-heavy (90% reads, 10% writes). Read replicas let you scale reads without touching your primary database.

```mermaid
graph TB
    App[Application] -->|Writes| Primary[(Primary DB)]
    App -->|Reads| R1[(Replica 1)]
    App -->|Reads| R2[(Replica 2)]
    App -->|Reads| R3[(Replica 3)]
    Primary -->|Replication| R1
    Primary -->|Replication| R2
    Primary -->|Replication| R3
```

### How Replication Works
1. App writes to Primary
2. Primary writes to its WAL (Write-Ahead Log)
3. Replicas consume the WAL and apply changes
4. Replicas are eventually consistent (lag: 10ms to seconds)

### Replication Lag Problem
```typescript
// User updates their name
await db.primary.query('UPDATE users SET name = ? WHERE id = ?', ['Bob', 1]);

// User immediately reads their profile (from replica)
const user = await db.replica.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', [1]);
// BUG: might still show old name due to replication lag!
```

**Solution**: Read-your-own-writes — after a write, read from primary for that user for the next few seconds.

### When to Use Read Replicas
- Read-heavy workloads (news feeds, product catalogs)
- Reporting/analytics queries (don't slow down production)
- Geographic distribution (replica in each region)

sessions.content:
## Master-Master Replication

What if you need writes in multiple locations? Master-master replication allows writes to ANY node.

```mermaid
graph LR
    App1[App US] -->|R/W| M1[(Master US)]
    App2[App EU] -->|R/W| M2[(Master EU)]
    M1 <-->|Sync| M2
```

### The Conflict Problem
What if two users update the same row on different masters at the same time?

```
Master US: UPDATE users SET name = 'Bob' WHERE id = 1  (at T1)
Master EU: UPDATE users SET name = 'Robert' WHERE id = 1  (at T1)
```

Who wins? This is the **write conflict** problem.

### Conflict Resolution Strategies
1. **Last-write-wins (LWW)**: Use timestamps. Latest write wins. Simple but can lose data.
2. **Application-level resolution**: App detects conflict and merges (like Git merge)
3. **CRDTs**: Special data structures that automatically merge without conflicts

### Interview Tip
'I'd avoid master-master replication unless absolutely necessary. The complexity of conflict resolution is significant. Instead, I'd use master-slave with geographic routing — users in the US always write to the US master, users in EU write to the EU master, based on data ownership.'

sessions.content:
## Sharding: Horizontal Partitioning

When a single database can't handle the load, you split the data across multiple databases. Each piece is called a **shard**.

```mermaid
graph TB
    App[Application] --> Router[Shard Router]
    Router --> S1[(Shard 1: Users A-H)]
    Router --> S2[(Shard 2: Users I-P)]
    Router --> S3[(Shard 3: Users Q-Z)]
```

### Types of Sharding

**Range-based**: Split by ranges of a key
```
Shard 1: user_id 1 - 1,000,000
Shard 2: user_id 1,000,001 - 2,000,000
Shard 3: user_id 2,000,001 - 3,000,000
```
Pro: Simple. Con: Hot spots (new users always hit the last shard).

**Hash-based**: Hash the key, mod by number of shards
```python
shard_number = hash(user_id) % num_shards
```
Pro: Even distribution. Con: Adding shards requires rehashing everything.

**Geographic**: Split by location
```
Shard US: All US users
Shard EU: All EU users
Shard Asia: All Asian users
```
Pro: Low latency (data near users). Con: Uneven shard sizes.

### The Golden Rule
Choose a shard key that:
1. Evenly distributes data
2. Evenly distributes queries
3. Avoids cross-shard queries

sessions.title: Consistent Hashing
sessions.paretoJustification: Range sharding enables efficient range queries; geographic sharding is essential for global apps

activities.description: Design range sharding for time-series events: shard by month. Plan migration strategy.
Design geographic sharding for a ride-sharing app considering GDPR EU data residency

sessions.content:
## Consistent Hashing

Regular hash-based sharding breaks when you add/remove shards (everything needs rehashing). Consistent hashing solves this.

### The Problem with Naive Hashing
```
3 shards: hash(key) % 3
  key=7 → 7%3 = 1 → Shard 1
  key=8 → 8%3 = 2 → Shard 2

Add 4th shard: hash(key) % 4
  key=7 → 7%4 = 3 → Shard 3 (MOVED!)
  key=8 → 8%4 = 0 → Shard 0 (MOVED!)
```
Almost ALL keys move! This is catastrophic for caches.

### How Consistent Hashing Works

Imagine a circle (hash ring) with positions 0 to 360:

1. Hash each server to a position on the ring
2. Hash each key to a position on the ring
3. Each key goes to the NEXT server clockwise

```
       Server A (90°)
      /
     /
(0°)-------- Key X (70°) → goes to Server A
     \
      \
       Server B (180°)
```

### Adding a Server
Only keys between the new server and the previous server need to move. ~1/N of keys instead of almost all.

### Virtual Nodes
Problem: With few servers, distribution is uneven.
Solution: Each server gets 100-200 virtual nodes spread around the ring.

### Real-World Usage
- Amazon DynamoDB
- Apache Cassandra
- Memcached
- Content Delivery Networks

sessions.content:
## Shard Key Selection

Choosing the right shard key is THE most important decision in sharding. Get it wrong, and you'll spend months migrating.

### Bad Shard Keys

**Timestamp**: All recent data on one shard → hot spot
```
Shard by created_at → New posts ALWAYS go to the latest shard
Result: One shard at 100% load, others idle
```

**Country**: Uneven distribution
```
Shard by country → India shard has 500M users, Luxembourg has 600K
```

### Good Shard Keys

**user_id**: Even distribution, most queries include user_id
```sql
-- Always hits exactly one shard
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = 12345;
```

**Compound key**: (tenant_id, created_at) for multi-tenant SaaS

### Shard Key Checklist
- [ ] High cardinality (many distinct values)
- [ ] Even distribution (no hot spots)
- [ ] Present in most queries (avoids scatter-gather)
- [ ] Immutable (never changes after creation)
- [ ] Monotonically increasing? NO (causes hot spots)

### Interview Scenario
'For a social media app, I'd shard by user_id. This means a user's posts, likes, and comments are all on the same shard. The trade-off is that building a global trending feed requires aggregating across all shards — but that's an acceptable background job.'

Design archival strategy: move old partitions to cold storage automatically

sessions.content:
## Cross-Shard Queries

The biggest challenge with sharding: queries that need data from multiple shards.

### Scatter-Gather Pattern
```
Query: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE total > 100 ORDER BY created_at LIMIT 10

1. Send query to ALL shards (scatter)
2. Each shard returns its top 10 results
3. Application merges and re-sorts all results (gather)
4. Return the final top 10
```

This is expensive — N shards = N queries. But sometimes unavoidable.

### Strategies to Minimize Cross-Shard Queries

**1. Denormalize**: Copy frequently-joined data into each shard
```
Instead of: orders shard + users shard (cross-shard JOIN)
Do: Store user_name, user_email directly in the orders table
```

**2. Application-level joins**
```typescript
// Step 1: Get order from orders shard
const order = await ordersShard.query('SELECT * FROM orders WHERE id = ?', [orderId]);
// Step 2: Get user from users shard
const user = await usersShard.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', [order.userId]);
// Step 3: Combine in application
return { ...order, userName: user.name };
```

**3. Global tables**: Some small tables (countries, categories) are replicated to every shard.

### Interview Tip
'I'd design the shard key so that 95% of queries hit a single shard. For the remaining 5% (reports, analytics), I'd use a read-optimized data store like Elasticsearch or a data warehouse.'

sessions.content:
## Database Partitioning Strategies

Partitioning is sharding within a single database — splitting a large table into smaller pieces on the same machine.

### Horizontal Partitioning (Most Common)
Split rows into separate physical tables.
```sql
-- PostgreSQL table partitioning
CREATE TABLE orders (
  id BIGSERIAL,
  user_id BIGINT,
  created_at TIMESTAMP,
  total DECIMAL(10,2)
) PARTITION BY RANGE (created_at);

CREATE TABLE orders_2024 PARTITION OF orders
  FOR VALUES FROM ('2024-01-01') TO ('2025-01-01');

CREATE TABLE orders_2025 PARTITION OF orders
  FOR VALUES FROM ('2025-01-01') TO ('2026-01-01');
```

### Benefits
- **Query speed**: Queries with partition key skip irrelevant partitions
- **Maintenance**: Drop old partitions instead of DELETE (instant vs hours)
- **Parallel I/O**: Different partitions on different disks

### Vertical Partitioning
Split columns into separate tables.
```sql
-- Frequently accessed columns
CREATE TABLE users_core (id, name, email);
-- Rarely accessed columns  
CREATE TABLE users_profile (id, bio, avatar_url, settings_json);
```

### When to Partition
- Table has >100 million rows
- Queries frequently filter by a specific column (date, region)
- You need to archive or delete old data efficiently

Implement double-write: write to old and new shard during migration, then cut over

reviewQuestions: What is the double-write pattern and when is it used?
