observableSkills: Implement bulkhead with separate thread pools
Implement back-pressure in producer-consumer
Apply all 12-factor principles to an application

milestoneProject.title: Resilient Scalable Service
milestoneProject.description: Build a service with: stateless design, Redis session store, connection pool, separate thread pools per dependency, back-pressure on queue, health endpoint.

levels.name: Proficient
levels.description: Designs multi-layer scalability (CDN + LB + auto-scale + cache + DB replicas), optimizes for cost
observableSkills: Design complete scaling architecture for 10M DAU
Optimize auto-scaling for cost (scale down aggressively)
Implement predictive scaling for known patterns

milestoneProject.title: Scale to 10M Users Design
milestoneProject.description: Design and document complete scaling architecture for a social app going from 10k to 10M DAU. Include CDN, LB, auto-scale groups, cache, read replicas. Estimate cost at each scale point.

levels.name: Expert
levels.description: Reasons about cell-based architecture, designs for chaos, implements scaling feedback loops
observableSkills: Design cell-based architecture for blast radius isolation
Implement chaos engineering for scaling validation
Design system that pre-scales based on ML predictions

milestoneProject.title: Planet-Scale Architecture
milestoneProject.description: Design a system architecture for 1 billion users. Cell-based deployment, global load balancing, predictive auto-scaling. Justify every trade-off with cost and reliability data.

plan.overview: Scalability is about delaying the need to rewrite your system. The mental model: scale up first (simple), then scale out (requires stateless design), then add caching (reduce load), then shard (last resort). Each step adds complexity. Add only when needed.
plan.skippedTopics: We skip cell-based architecture in depth (very advanced, Google/AWS-specific). We skip FPGA/hardware acceleration. We focus on software patterns applicable to most systems.

sessions.title: Scale Up vs Scale Out
sessions.paretoJustification: This is the first decision in any scalability conversation
objectives: Quantify the limits of vertical scaling
Identify when horizontal scaling is required

activities.description: Research: largest available AWS instance. What are its limits vs what a horizontal cluster can do?
Design: what code changes are required to go from 1 server to 10 servers?

reviewQuestions: When is vertical scaling the right first move?
What is the largest vertical size you can get on AWS?
What must change in your application to horizontally scale?
sessions.successCriteria: Can articulate when each approach is appropriate with cost and complexity justification
sessions.hookQuestion: Your API server uses 100% CPU at 1,000 requests per second. You can buy a 2x faster server OR buy a second identical server. Which scales better long-term, and why?
sessions.realWorldMotivation: Every startup hits this exact decision at 10k, 100k, and 1M users. The wrong choice either costs 10x in infrastructure or requires rewriting the entire application. Netflix, Uber, and Airbnb all made this decision and documented the results.
keyTakeaways: Vertical scaling cost is super-linear above 4x baseline — at 50x capacity, horizontal is ~45x cheaper.
Stateless = any server can serve any request. Move sessions to Redis, files to S3, jobs to SQS.
Auto-scaling lag: EC2 needs 90-180 seconds to start. Always maintain 20-30% spare capacity to absorb spikes during scale-up.
Start vertical (simplicity), scale horizontal when cost becomes super-linear or SPOF risk is unacceptable.
sessions.content:
## Horizontal vs Vertical Scaling — The Core Trade-off ### Vertical Scaling (Scale Up) Add more resources to a single machine: more CPU cores, RAM, faster storage. **Advantages**: - Zero application changes required - No distributed systems complexity - Shared memory — no serialization overhead between components - Strong consistency trivially (single node) **Disadvantages**: - Hard upper limit: largest AWS instance is `u-24tb1.metal` — 448 vCPUs, 24 TB RAM, ~$220/hour - Single point of failure — one machine crash = total outage - Cost increases super-linearly: 2x resources ≈ 4-8x cost at the high end - Maintenance window requires full downtime ```python # Modelling vertical vs horizontal cost curves def vertical_cost(baseline_rps: int, target_rps: int, baseline_cost: float) -> float: scale_factor = target_rps / baseline_rps if scale_factor <= 4: return baseline_cost * scale_factor * 1.5 # premium per unit return baseline_cost * scale_factor ** 2 # super-linear at high end def horizontal_cost(baseline_rps: int, target_rps: int, baseline_cost: float) -> float: scale_factor = target_rps / baseline_rps coordination_overhead = 1.10 # 10% for load balancer, session mgmt return baseline_cost * scale_factor * coordination_overhead baseline_rps = 1_000 baseline_cost = 100 # $/month for target in [2_000, 5_000, 10_000, 50_000]: v = vertical_cost(baseline_rps, target, baseline_cost) h = horizontal_cost(baseline_rps, target, baseline_cost) print(f"Target {target:6} RPS: Vertical=${v:8,.0f} Horizontal=${h:8,.0f} Ratio={v/h:.1f}x") # Target 2000 RPS: Vertical=$ 300 Horizontal=$ 220 Ratio=1.4x # Target 5000 RPS: Vertical=$ 750 Horizontal=$ 550 Ratio=1.4x # Target 50000 RPS: Vertical=$250,000 Horizontal=$5,500 Ratio=45.5x ``` ### Horizontal Scaling (Scale Out) Add more machines of the same size, distribute load across them. **Advantages**: - Theoretically unlimited scale - No single point of failure - Cost scales linearly with capacity **Disadvantages**: - Application must be stateless - Added latency for inter-node communication (1–10 ms vs nanoseconds in-memory) - Operational complexity: service discovery, load balancing, distributed tracing ## Stateless Services — The Key to Horizontal Scaling A stateless service stores **no session state in memory**. Every request carries all the information needed to process it, or the service fetches state from a shared external store. ```typescript // BAD: STATEFUL — cannot horizontally scale (session pinned to server) const sessionStore = new Map(); // in-memory app.post("/api/cart/add", (req, res) => { const session = sessionStore.get(req.headers["session-id"] as string); // If request hits a different server, sessionStore is empty! session!.cart.push(req.body.item); res.json({ cart: session!.cart }); }); // GOOD: STATELESS — load balancer can route to any server app.post("/api/cart/add", async (req, res) => { const sessionId = req.headers["session-id"] as string; // All state in Redis — any server can serve any request const raw = await redis.get(`cart:${sessionId}`); const cart: string[] = raw ? JSON.parse(raw) : []; cart.push(req.body.item); await redis.set(`cart:${sessionId}`, JSON.stringify(cart), "EX", 3600); res.json({ cart }); }); ``` ### What Must Move Out of the Application Server | State Type | Move To | |-----------|----------| | User sessions | Redis / DynamoDB | | File uploads | S3 / GCS | | Scheduled jobs | SQS + worker fleet | | WebSocket connections | Redis Pub/Sub or dedicated gateway | | Local cache | Shared Redis or CDN | ## Auto-Scaling ```mermaid graph TD CW["CloudWatch Metrics
CPU > 70% for 2 min"] -->|alarm| ASG["Auto Scaling Group"] ASG -->|launch| EC2_1["EC2 Instance 1"] ASG -->|launch| EC2_2["EC2 Instance 2"] ASG -->|launch| EC2_3["EC2 Instance 3 (new)"] LB["Application Load Balancer"] --> EC2_1 LB --> EC2_2 LB --> EC2_3 style EC2_3 fill:#27ae60,color:#fff ``` **Key auto-scaling numbers**: - EC2 instance launch: **90–180 seconds** (AMI + user-data boot) - Kubernetes pod scale-up: **5–30 seconds** (image cached) or **2–5 minutes** (image pull) - Lambda cold start: **100–500 ms** (Python/Node) or **1–10 s** (JVM) This means auto-scaling **cannot react to sudden traffic spikes** — you need pre-warming or buffer capacity (20–30% headroom). ```python import math def required_capacity( expected_peak_rps: int, single_instance_rps: int, scale_up_lag_seconds: int = 120, traffic_growth_per_second: float = 50, ) -> dict: traffic_increase_during_lag = traffic_growth_per_second * scale_up_lag_seconds total_rps_at_end_of_lag = expected_peak_rps + traffic_increase_during_lag instances_needed = math.ceil(total_rps_at_end_of_lag / single_instance_rps) buffer_instances = math.ceil(expected_peak_rps / single_instance_rps) return { "peak_rps": expected_peak_rps, "instances_for_peak_only": buffer_instances, "instances_with_lag_buffer": instances_needed, "extra_buffer_instances": instances_needed - buffer_instances, } result = required_capacity( expected_peak_rps=10_000, single_instance_rps=500, scale_up_lag_seconds=120, traffic_growth_per_second=100, ) print(result) # {'peak_rps': 10000, 'instances_for_peak_only': 20, # 'instances_with_lag_buffer': 44, 'extra_buffer_instances': 24} ``` ## Interview Q&A **Q: When would you use vertical scaling over horizontal?** A: Early stage (< 10k RPS), stateful applications that are expensive to refactor, databases that do not shard well, or when operational simplicity outweighs cost. Always start vertical, scale horizontal when you hit the ceiling. **Q: How do you handle WebSocket connections during horizontal scaling?** A: Use a sticky load balancer (IP hash or cookie-based) for the connection, plus Redis Pub/Sub for cross-node message delivery. Or use a dedicated WebSocket gateway (AWS API Gateway WebSocket, Socket.io with Redis adapter). **Q: What is the thundering herd problem in auto-scaling?** A: When a spike hits, all waiting requests pile up. New instances come online and immediately receive a tsunami of queued requests. Mitigate with request queuing, circuit breakers, and gradual traffic shifting.

sessions.title: Stateless Services
sessions.paretoJustification: Statelessness is the prerequisite for all horizontal scaling

activities.description: Audit a sample application: what state exists? Where is it stored?
Move in-memory sessions to Redis, JWT for auth state in token

reviewQuestions: What state cannot be made stateless?
How do WebSocket connections complicate statelessness?
What is a sticky session and why is it a code smell?
sessions.successCriteria: Can refactor any stateful service to stateless with Redis-backed state
sessions.hookQuestion: Your database has 10,000 idle connections from 500 app servers — each server has a pool of 20. The database crashes. What happens when all 500 servers try to reconnect simultaneously?
sessions.realWorldMotivation: Connection pool exhaustion has taken down Shopify, GitHub, and Instagram in production. A misconfigured pool size causes more outages than almost any other single configuration error.
keyTakeaways: PostgreSQL optimal pool size: (cores × 2) + 1 for SSDs. A 32-core server should have ~65 connections, not 10,000.
PgBouncer in transaction mode: 500 app servers × 20 app-pool connections can share just 65 actual DB connections.
Back-pressure: when downstream is saturated, reject new requests fast (503) rather than queuing indefinitely — prevents memory exhaustion.
Bulkhead: give payments, search, and analytics separate thread pools so a slow analytics query never starves payment processing.

sessions.title: Auto-Scaling
sessions.paretoJustification: Auto-scaling is the operational realization of horizontal scaling

activities.description: Configure AWS Auto Scaling Group with CPU-based scaling policy
Load test: measure how long from scale event to new instances handling traffic

reviewQuestions: What is the warm-up period for a new instance?
Why is predictive scaling better for known patterns?
What metric should you scale on for a queue-backed service?
sessions.successCriteria: Working auto-scaling group that scales out under load and scales in during quiet periods
sessions.hookQuestion: Your startup has 1 million users. Tomorrow you will be on the front page of TechCrunch and expect 10 million by the end of the day. You have 12 hours. What do you do first?
sessions.realWorldMotivation: Database scaling is the most common bottleneck at every scale inflection point. Reddit scaled from 1M to 1B pageviews/month almost entirely through database optimisation before introducing microservices. Instagram served 1M users on 3 engineers through smart sharding.
keyTakeaways: 80% of database scaling problems are solved by: adding a read replica + query caching (95% cache hit = 95% less DB load).
Consistent hashing for sharding: adding 1 shard to a 3-shard cluster migrates only ~25% of keys, vs 100% with mod sharding.
CDN cache hits: static assets in 5ms vs 85ms+ to origin. stale-while-revalidate lets CDN serve fast AND keep content fresh.
Capacity formula: peak_rps = (DAU × requests/day) / 86400 × 3. Always plan for 3x average as peak headroom.

sessions.title: Queue-Based Load Leveling
sessions.paretoJustification: Queues absorb spikes — critical for high-variability workloads

activities.description: Convert synchronous API call to async: API → SQS → worker → DB
Configure SQS-based auto-scaling: scale workers based on queue depth

reviewQuestions: What is queue depth-based auto-scaling?
How do you communicate async result back to client?
When is synchronous better than async?
sessions.successCriteria: Working async processing pipeline with queue-based worker auto-scaling
sessions.hookQuestion: Your service handles 50,000 API requests/second. Suddenly one customer sends 10,000 requests/second — a bug in their code. Without rate limiting, what happens to your other 999 customers?
sessions.realWorldMotivation: Stripe, Twilio, and GitHub all publish their rate limit designs. Token bucket and sliding window algorithms appear in every senior backend interview. Getting this wrong costs customers — and potentially your job.
keyTakeaways: Token bucket allows bursting up to bucket capacity, then sustained rate. Leaky bucket smooths output rate. Sliding window is most accurate but memory-intensive.
Redis INCR + EXPIRE implements fixed-window rate limiting in 2 commands. Sliding window requires a sorted set — O(log N) per request.
Rate limit headers: X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset, Retry-After. Clients that respect these headers avoid unnecessary 429s.
Multi-tier rate limiting: global limit at API gateway, per-customer at service layer, per-endpoint at route level. Each tier protects against different attack patterns.

sessions.title: Bulkhead Pattern
sessions.paretoJustification: Bulkheads prevent one slow dependency from bringing down all functionality

activities.description: Implement separate thread pools for 3 downstream services in Java/Go
Simulate: payment service slow (pool full) — verify inventory and shipping still work

reviewQuestions: How do you size each bulkhead pool?
What is the overhead of separate thread pools?
How does bulkhead relate to circuit breaker?
sessions.successCriteria: Can demonstrate that one saturated pool doesn't affect others
sessions.hookQuestion: A single SQL query reads 1 million rows to compute a dashboard metric that 10,000 users check every minute. How many times does this query actually need to run per minute?
sessions.realWorldMotivation: Caching is responsible for making YouTube, Twitter, and Facebook fast. Redis handles 1 million operations/second on a single node. Understanding when to cache, what to cache, and how to invalidate is the difference between a system that scales and one that doesn't.
keyTakeaways: Cache-aside (lazy loading): cache miss triggers DB fetch + cache populate. Simple but has cold start problem. TTL handles staleness.
Write-through: every write goes to cache and DB simultaneously. No stale reads, but write latency doubles. Good for read-heavy, write-rare data.
Write-behind (write-back): write to cache immediately, async to DB. Fast writes, but data loss risk if cache fails before flush.
Cache stampede prevention: use probabilistic early expiration or a distributed lock (Redis SETNX) so only one process refills the cache.

sessions.title: Back-Pressure
sessions.paretoJustification: Back-pressure prevents overload propagation — essential for stable systems

activities.description: Implement buffered channel with back-pressure in Go: producer slows when buffer full
Design: what does the client see when back-pressure is applied? 429 with Retry-After?

reviewQuestions: How does TCP back-pressure work?
What is the difference between back-pressure and rate limiting?
How do you surface back-pressure to clients?
sessions.successCriteria: Working producer-consumer with measurable back-pressure under load
sessions.hookQuestion: Your app serves images to 100 million users globally. A user in Sydney downloads a 2MB image stored in US-East. The latency is 280ms. A CDN edge node exists 10ms away in Sydney. What changes?
sessions.realWorldMotivation: Cloudflare serves 45 million HTTP requests per second from 285 cities. Fastly powers Stripe, GitHub, and The New York Times. CDN architecture decisions impact user experience, SEO rankings (Core Web Vitals), and infrastructure cost more than almost any other single choice.
keyTakeaways: CDN cache hit: 10ms (Sydney edge). CDN cache miss: 10 + 280 + 280 + 10 = 580ms (full origin round-trip). Cache-hit ratio of 90%+ is the CDN goal.
Cache-Control: public max-age=31536000 immutable for versioned assets. stale-while-revalidate=60 for API responses needing freshness.
Cache key design: include Accept-Encoding, Vary: Accept-Encoding header. CDN Vary header multiplies cache entries per value.
Origin shield: single POP that all edges check before hitting origin. Reduces origin load from 285 POPs to 1 cache miss per item.

sessions.title: Connection Pooling at Scale
sessions.paretoJustification: Connection exhaustion is a silent killer as you horizontally scale

activities.description: Calculate: 50 app servers × 20 connections = 1000 DB connections. PgBouncer reduces to 20.
Deploy PgBouncer, configure transaction mode pooling, measure connection count

reviewQuestions: What is transaction mode vs session mode in PgBouncer?
What happens to prepared statements in transaction mode?
How do you monitor connection pool health?
sessions.successCriteria: Working PgBouncer reducing connection count by 10x under the same load
sessions.hookQuestion: Black Friday is in 3 months. Last year your system collapsed at 10x normal traffic. You need to guarantee it won't happen again. What is your plan?
sessions.realWorldMotivation: Amazon estimates every 100ms of latency costs 1% of revenue. Walmart lost $1.5B in a single day to a website outage during a sale. Capacity planning separates companies that survive hypergrowth from those that don't.
keyTakeaways: Load testing formula: simulate 3x expected peak with ramp-up. Find the breaking point, then fix and re-test. Never go to production without a known breaking point.
Little's Law: L = lambda * W. If average response time (W) doubles, queue depth (L) doubles at the same arrival rate (lambda). Latency drives capacity.
SLO error budget: 99.9% uptime = 43.8 minutes downtime/month. When budget is exhausted, freeze feature work until reliability is restored.
Capacity model: baseline + growth + seasonal spikes + safety margin (20%). Track unit economics: cost per request, cost per user, to catch regressions early.
sessions.content:
## Capacity Planning — From Art to Science ### The Capacity Planning Cycle ```mermaid graph LR A["1. Measure
Current baseline"] --> B["2. Model
Growth projections"] B --> C["3. Test
Load testing"] C --> D["4. Plan
Infrastructure changes"] D --> E["5. Monitor
Track actuals vs model"] E --> A ``` ### Step 1 — Measure Your Baseline ```python from dataclasses import dataclass from typing import Optional import math @dataclass class SystemMetrics: avg_rps: float p50_latency_ms: float p99_latency_ms: float cpu_utilisation: float # 0-1 memory_utilisation: float # 0-1 error_rate: float # 0-1 instances: int def headroom(self) -> dict: cpu_headroom = 1 - self.cpu_utilisation return { "cpu_headroom": f"{cpu_headroom:.0%}", "safe_rps_multiplier": cpu_headroom / 0.7, # keep CPU < 70% "estimated_breaking_point_rps": self.avg_rps / (self.cpu_utilisation / 0.9), } baseline = SystemMetrics( avg_rps=5_000, p50_latency_ms=45, p99_latency_ms=250, cpu_utilisation=0.35, memory_utilisation=0.60, error_rate=0.001, instances=10, ) headroom = baseline.headroom() print(f"Current CPU: {baseline.cpu_utilisation:.0%}") print(f"Safe to 70% CPU: {headroom['safe_rps_multiplier']:.1f}x current load") print(f"Estimated break point: {headroom['estimated_breaking_point_rps']:,.0f} RPS") # Current CPU: 35% # Safe to 70% CPU: 1.0x current load # Estimated break point: 12,857 RPS ``` ### Little's Law — Latency Drives Queue Depth **L = lambda × W** (queue depth = arrival rate × average service time) ```python def littles_law_analysis(arrival_rate_rps: float, latency_ms: float) -> dict: queue_depth = arrival_rate_rps * (latency_ms / 1000) return { "arrival_rate_rps": arrival_rate_rps, "avg_latency_ms": latency_ms, "avg_queue_depth": round(queue_depth, 1), "implication": f"System has {queue_depth:.0f} requests in flight on average", } # Normal operation normal = littles_law_analysis(arrival_rate_rps=1000, latency_ms=50) print(normal) # avg_queue_depth: 50.0 — manageable # Latency degrades (DB slow query) — same traffic degraded = littles_law_analysis(arrival_rate_rps=1000, latency_ms=500) print(degraded) # avg_queue_depth: 500.0 — 10x queue depth from 10x latency! # If thread pool = 200, system is now saturated and rejecting requests ``` ### Step 2 — Load Testing ```typescript // k6 load test script import http from "k6/http"; import { check, sleep } from "k6"; import { Rate, Trend } from "k6/metrics"; const errorRate = new Rate("errors"); const p99Latency = new Trend("p99_latency"); export const options = { stages: [ { duration: "2m", target: 100 }, // warm up { duration: "5m", target: 1000 }, // normal load { duration: "5m", target: 3000 }, // 3x peak (Black Friday) { duration: "5m", target: 5000 }, // push to breaking point { duration: "2m", target: 0 }, // cool down ], thresholds: { http_req_duration: ["p(99)<500"], // p99 must be < 500ms errors: ["rate<0.01"], // error rate < 1% }, }; export default function () { const response = http.get("https://api.example.com/products", { headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${__ENV.TEST_TOKEN}` }, }); check(response, { "status 200": (r) => r.status === 200, "response time < 200ms": (r) => r.timings.duration < 200, }); errorRate.add(response.status !== 200); p99Latency.add(response.timings.duration); sleep(1); } ``` ### Step 3 — Capacity Model ```python def capacity_model( current_rps: float, monthly_growth_rate: float, months: int, peak_multiplier: float = 3.0, safety_margin: float = 0.20, cost_per_instance_monthly: float = 200, rps_per_instance: float = 500, ) -> list: results = [] rps = current_rps for month in range(months + 1): peak_rps = rps * peak_multiplier required_with_safety = peak_rps * (1 + safety_margin) instances = math.ceil(required_with_safety / rps_per_instance) results.append({ "month": month, "avg_rps": round(rps), "peak_rps": round(peak_rps), "instances": instances, "monthly_cost": instances * cost_per_instance_monthly, }) rps *= (1 + monthly_growth_rate) return results forecast = capacity_model( current_rps=5_000, monthly_growth_rate=0.10, # 10% MoM growth months=6, ) for row in forecast: print(f"Month {row['month']}: avg={row['avg_rps']:,} peak={row['peak_rps']:,} " f"instances={row['instances']} cost=${row['monthly_cost']:,}") ``` ### SLO Error Budget ```python def slo_error_budget(target_availability: float, period_days: int = 30) -> dict: downtime_minutes = (1 - target_availability) * period_days * 24 * 60 error_budget_requests_pct = (1 - target_availability) * 100 return { "target": f"{target_availability:.3%}", "allowed_downtime_minutes": round(downtime_minutes, 1), "error_budget_pct": f"{error_budget_requests_pct:.4f}%", } print(slo_error_budget(0.999)) # 99.9%: 43.8 min/month print(slo_error_budget(0.9999)) # 99.99%: 4.4 min/month print(slo_error_budget(0.99999)) # 99.999%: 0.44 min/month ``` ## Interview Q&A **Q: How do you capacity plan for an unknown traffic spike (product launch)?** A: (1) Pre-scale to 3x normal capacity before the event. (2) Enable auto-scaling with aggressive scale-out policy. (3) Load test to known breaking point. (4) Implement request queuing and graceful degradation so partial failures don't cascade. (5) War room with monitoring during the event. **Q: What is an error budget and how do you enforce it?** A: Error budget = allowed failures within an SLO period. When budget runs low, feature work pauses until reliability is restored. Teams burn budget intentionally through chaos testing; unexpected burns trigger postmortems and reliability work. **Q: How do you handle unexpected 10x traffic (viral moment)?** A: Short term: serve cached responses aggressively, enable feature flags to disable expensive features, increase cache TTLs, trigger auto-scaling. Medium term: add CDN caching for previously dynamic content. If still failing: graceful degradation (show simplified page) or shed load with 503. ## Chaos Engineering — Validating Your Capacity Plan Load testing tells you how your system behaves under expected conditions. Chaos engineering tests unexpected conditions: network failures, slow dependencies, disk full, clock skew. ```python import random, asyncio class ChaosMiddleware: def __init__(self, failure_rate: float = 0.01): self.failure_rate = failure_rate async def latency_injection(self, func, min_ms=100, max_ms=5000): if random.random() < self.failure_rate: delay = random.uniform(min_ms, max_ms) / 1000 await asyncio.sleep(delay) return await func() chaos = ChaosMiddleware(failure_rate=0.05) # 5% during testing async def get_user_with_chaos(user_id: str): return await chaos.latency_injection(lambda: fetch_user(user_id)) ``` ### The Disaster Recovery Runbook Every capacity plan must include a tested runbook for common failure scenarios. Runbooks that have never been executed in a drill will fail when you need them: ```python RUNBOOK = { "database_primary_failure": { "detection": "pg_stat_replication lag > 60s OR primary unreachable for 30s", "steps": [ "1. Verify: pg_isready -h primary-host (should fail)", "2. Promote standby: pg_ctl promote -D /var/lib/postgresql/data", "3. Update Route53 CNAME: primary -> new-primary (TTL 60s, takes 60s to propagate)", "4. Verify reconnect: watch db_pool_active in Grafana (should recover within 2 min)", "5. Monitor for 15 minutes: error rate, latency, replication lag on new primary", ], "rto_target_minutes": 5, "last_tested": "2024-01-15", }, } ``` ### SLO Error Budget in Practice ```python def error_budget_status(target: float, actual_errors: int, total_requests: int, period_days: int = 30) -> dict: allowed_error_rate = 1 - target actual_error_rate = actual_errors / max(1, total_requests) budget_consumed = actual_error_rate / allowed_error_rate return { "target_availability": f"{target:.3%}", "actual_error_rate": f"{actual_error_rate:.4%}", "budget_consumed_pct": f"{budget_consumed:.0%}", "status": "BURN" if budget_consumed > 0.5 else "OK", } print(error_budget_status(0.999, 1500, 1_000_000)) # target: 99.900%, error_rate: 0.1500%, budget_consumed: 150%, status: BURN # 150% budget consumed in <30 days means SLO already breached ``` When the error budget is exhausted, the engineering contract is clear: **freeze feature work** and focus entirely on reliability until the budget is restored. This makes SLOs a forcing function for reliability investment.

sessions.title: Designing Scalable Systems - The Scale Cube
sessions.paretoJustification: The Scale Cube provides a framework for systematically addressing scaling needs

activities.description: Apply Scale Cube to an e-commerce site: X-axis (clone), Y-axis (microservices), Z-axis (shard)
For each axis: what problem does it solve? What complexity does it add?

reviewQuestions: What does X-axis scaling require (statelessness)?
How does Y-axis (microservices) enable independent scaling?
When do you use Z-axis (data partitioning)?
sessions.successCriteria: Can apply Scale Cube framework to any system and prescribe the right axis
sessions.hookQuestion: A deploy goes wrong at 2 AM. Your service is throwing 50% errors. You have 5 minutes before your SLA breach triggers a financial penalty. What exact sequence of actions do you take?
sessions.realWorldMotivation: Observability is what separates a 1-hour outage from a 10-minute one. Google SRE practices, OpenTelemetry, and the three pillars of observability (metrics, logs, traces) are standard topics in principal/staff engineer interviews.
keyTakeaways: Three pillars: metrics (what is wrong), logs (why it is wrong), traces (where in the request flow it is wrong). All three are needed for fast diagnosis.
USE method: Utilisation, Saturation, Errors — apply to every resource (CPU, memory, network, disk, thread pools, connection pools).
Distributed tracing: every request gets a trace ID at entry. Spans cover each service/DB call. P99 span duration identifies the bottleneck service.
Alert on symptoms (user-visible errors, latency), not causes (CPU > 80%). Cause-based alerts are noisy and miss novel failure modes.
sessions.content:
## Observability — The Three Pillars

Observability = the ability to understand a system's internal state from its external outputs. Without it, you are operating blind.

### Pillar 1: Metrics

Metrics answer: **what is wrong right now?**

The **Four Golden Signals** (Google SRE Book):
1. **Latency**: time to service requests (p50, p95, p99)
2. **Traffic**: demand on the system (RPS, active users)
3. **Errors**: rate of failed requests (5xx, timeout, client errors)
4. **Saturation**: how "full" the system is (CPU %, queue depth, connection pool utilisation)

```python
from prometheus_client import Counter, Histogram, Gauge, start_http_server
import time

# Prometheus metrics
request_duration = Histogram(
    "http_request_duration_seconds",
    "Request duration in seconds",
    ["method", "endpoint", "status_code"],
    buckets=[0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.5, 5.0],
)

request_total = Counter(
    "http_requests_total",
    "Total HTTP requests",
    ["method", "endpoint", "status_code"],
)

db_pool_utilisation = Gauge(
    "db_pool_utilisation",
    "Fraction of DB pool connections in use",
)

def track_request(method: str, endpoint: str):
    def decorator(func):
        def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
            start = time.time()
            status = "500"
            try:
                result = func(*args, **kwargs)
                status = "200"
                return result
            except Exception:
                raise
            finally:
                duration = time.time() - start
                request_duration.labels(method, endpoint, status).observe(duration)
                request_total.labels(method, endpoint, status).inc()
        return wrapper
    return decorator

@track_request("GET", "/api/products")
def get_products():
    return db.query("SELECT * FROM products LIMIT 100")
```

### Pillar 2: Logs

Logs answer: **why is it wrong?**

```typescript
import pino from "pino";

const logger = pino({
  level: process.env.LOG_LEVEL || "info",
  base: {
    service: "product-api",
    version: process.env.APP_VERSION,
    env: process.env.NODE_ENV,
  },
  // Structured logging: every field is queryable
  serializers: {
    req: (req) => ({
      method: req.method,
      url: req.url,
      traceId: req.headers["x-trace-id"], // correlate with traces
    }),
    err: pino.stdSerializers.err,
  },
});

// Always log with context — correlatable with trace ID
async function handleProductRequest(req: any, res: any) {
  const log = logger.child({ traceId: req.headers["x-trace-id"], userId: req.user?.id });

  log.info({ productId: req.params.id }, "Fetching product");

  try {
    const product = await db.getProduct(req.params.id);
    log.info({ productId: product.id, cacheHit: product.fromCache }, "Product fetched");
    res.json(product);
  } catch (err) {
    log.error({ err, productId: req.params.id }, "Failed to fetch product");
    res.status(500).json({ error: "Internal Server Error" });
  }
}
```

### Pillar 3: Distributed Traces

Traces answer: **where in the request flow is it slow?**

```python
from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter
from opentelemetry.instrumentation.requests import RequestsInstrumentor
from opentelemetry.instrumentation.psycopg2 import Psycopg2Instrumentor

# Setup: auto-instrument HTTP and DB calls
provider = TracerProvider()
provider.add_span_processor(BatchSpanProcessor(OTLPSpanExporter(endpoint="http://jaeger:4317")))
trace.set_tracer_provider(provider)

# Auto-instrument (zero code change to existing functions)
RequestsInstrumentor().instrument()  # instruments all requests.get() calls
Psycopg2Instrumentor().instrument()  # instruments all DB queries

tracer = trace.get_tracer(__name__)

def process_order(order_id: str) -> dict:
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("process_order") as span:
        span.set_attribute("order.id", order_id)
        # Child spans automatically created for HTTP and DB calls
        order = fetch_order(order_id)           # auto-traced DB span
        inventory = check_inventory(order)       # auto-traced HTTP span
        charge_result = charge_payment(order)    # auto-traced HTTP span
        span.set_attribute("order.total", order["total"])
        return {"order_id": order_id, "status": "processed"}
```

### USE Method — Resource Health Checklist

For each resource (CPU, memory, disk, network, thread pool, connection pool):

```
Utilisation: what percentage is being used?
Saturation: is there a queue forming? (queue depth > 0)
Errors: are there errors specific to this resource?
```

```python
def use_health_check(metrics: dict) -> list:
    issues = []
    resources = [
        {"name": "CPU", "util": metrics["cpu_pct"], "sat": metrics["load_avg_1m"] / metrics["cpu_cores"]},
        {"name": "Memory", "util": metrics["memory_pct"], "sat": metrics.get("swap_used_pct", 0)},
        {"name": "DB Pool", "util": metrics["db_pool_used"] / metrics["db_pool_size"],
         "sat": metrics["db_pool_waiting"]},
        {"name": "Disk IO", "util": metrics["disk_util_pct"], "sat": metrics.get("disk_queue_depth", 0)},
    ]
    for r in resources:
        if r["util"] > 0.8:
            issues.append(f"HIGH: {r['name']} utilisation at {r['util']:.0%}")
        if r["sat"] > 0:
            issues.append(f"SATURATED: {r['name']} has queue forming")
    return issues
```

## Interview Q&A

**Q: What is the difference between monitoring and observability?**
A: Monitoring tells you when something is wrong (known failure modes). Observability lets you investigate unknown failure modes by exploring data. Monitoring = predefined dashboards + alerts. Observability = ad-hoc querying of metrics, logs, and traces to answer arbitrary questions.

**Q: How do you alert without alert fatigue?**
A: Alert on symptoms (user-visible): error rate > 1%, p99 latency > 500ms. Not on causes: CPU > 80% (may be fine), memory growing (may be cache). Use multi-window multi-burn-rate alerting for SLO-based alerts (alert when burn rate threatens to exhaust error budget).

**Q: What is exemplar-based monitoring?**
A: Attach a trace ID to a metric data point. When investigating a p99 latency spike on a dashboard, click the data point to jump directly to a trace that demonstrates the slow request. OpenTelemetry supports exemplars natively.

## SLO-Based Alerting — Multi-Window Multi-Burn-Rate

```python
def calculate_burn_rate(
    error_rate: float,
    slo_availability: float = 0.999,
    period_hours: float = 720,
) -> dict:
    error_budget = 1 - slo_availability
    burn_rate = error_rate / error_budget
    hours_until_exhausted = period_hours / burn_rate if burn_rate > 0 else float("inf")
    return {
        "burn_rate": round(burn_rate, 1),
        "budget_exhausted_hours": round(hours_until_exhausted, 1),
        "alert_level": "PAGE" if burn_rate >= 14.4 else "TICKET" if burn_rate >= 1 else "OK",
    }

print(calculate_burn_rate(0.0144, 0.999))  # burn_rate=14.4 -> PAGE (2 days)
print(calculate_burn_rate(0.001, 0.999))   # burn_rate=1.0  -> TICKET (consuming at SLO rate)
print(calculate_burn_rate(0.0001, 0.999))  # burn_rate=0.1  -> OK
```

| Window | Burn Rate | Alert Type | Rationale |
|--------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| 5 min + 1 hour | > 14.4x | Page | Budget gone in 2 days |
| 30 min + 6 hour | > 6x | Page | Budget gone in 5 days |
| 2 hour + 1 day | > 3x | Ticket | Budget gone in 10 days |
| 1 day + 3 day | > 1x | Ticket | Budget consuming |

This reduces alert noise by 90%+ vs threshold-based alerting while maintaining detection sensitivity for user-impacting issues.

### Exemplar-Based Monitoring

Attach a trace ID to metric data points. When investigating a p99 latency spike on a Grafana dashboard, click the data point to jump directly to a trace that demonstrates the slow request. OpenTelemetry supports exemplars natively:

```python
from prometheus_client import Histogram
from opentelemetry import trace

request_duration = Histogram(
    "http_request_duration_seconds",
    "Request duration",
    buckets=[0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.5, 5.0],
)

def track_with_exemplar(duration_seconds: float):
    current_span = trace.get_current_span()
    trace_id = format(current_span.get_span_context().trace_id, '032x')
    # Prometheus exemplar: metric data point links directly to trace
    request_duration.observe(duration_seconds, exemplar={"traceID": trace_id})
    # In Grafana: click the p99 spike -> see the exact trace ID -> open in Jaeger
```

### The USE Method Applied to Every Resource

For each resource in your system, track Utilisation, Saturation, and Errors:

| Resource | Utilisation Metric | Saturation Metric | Error Metric |
|----------|-------------------|------------------|--------------|
| CPU | cpu_usage_pct | load_avg / cpu_count | kernel_errors |
| Memory | memory_used_pct | swap_used_bytes | oom_kills |
| DB pool | pool_used / pool_max | pool_waiting_count | connection_errors |
| Network | bytes_out / bandwidth | packets_dropped | tcp_retransmits |
| Disk IO | io_util_pct | io_queue_depth | disk_errors |

sessions.title: Performance vs Scalability
sessions.paretoJustification: Understanding the distinction prevents solving the wrong problem

activities.description: Profile a sample application: find the hot path. Optimize before throwing hardware at it.
Quantify: if you optimize a query from 100ms to 10ms, how does this change scaling requirements?

reviewQuestions: Can a system be fast but not scalable?
Can a system be scalable but slow?
What is Amdahl's Law and how does it apply to parallelism?
sessions.successCriteria: Can distinguish performance vs scalability problems and recommend appropriate fixes
sessions.hookQuestion: Your monolith handles 5,000 requests per second comfortably. The team wants to break it into microservices. The architect says it will make the system more scalable. Will it? What are the actual trade-offs?
sessions.realWorldMotivation: Amazon, Netflix, and Uber all went from monolith to microservices. Segment and Prime Video went back. Understanding when microservices help (and hurt) is a core senior engineer competency.
keyTakeaways: Microservices add network latency (1-10ms per hop) and operational complexity. A 5-service call chain adds 5-50ms of pure overhead versus in-process calls.
Services communicate via REST/gRPC (synchronous) or message queues (asynchronous). Async decoupling improves resilience but adds eventual consistency.
Service mesh (Istio, Linkerd) handles: mTLS, retries, circuit breaking, distributed tracing — without modifying application code.
Decompose by bounded context (DDD), not by technical layer. Splitting at the wrong seam creates a distributed monolith — all the complexity, none of the benefits.

sessions.title: System Design Mock: Scalability
sessions.paretoJustification: Synthesis for interview performance

activities.description: Mock: Design a URL shortener that scales from 100 to 100M users
Mock: How would you handle Black Friday for an e-commerce site?

reviewQuestions: What is your bottleneck at each scale?
What does your system look like at 1k vs 1M vs 100M users?
How do you reduce cost during off-peak hours?
sessions.successCriteria: Phased scaling design that correctly identifies bottlenecks at each scale point
sessions.hookQuestion: Design Twitter's timeline service. A user has 50 million followers. They tweet. Within 5 seconds, all 50 million followers' timelines should reflect that tweet. How?
sessions.realWorldMotivation: Timeline generation is the canonical scalability design question. Twitter, Facebook, Instagram, and LinkedIn have all published detailed engineering blog posts about their approaches. Answering this demonstrates mastery of caching, fan-out, and real-time architecture.
keyTakeaways: Fan-out on write (push): precompute timelines on tweet creation. O(followers) writes per tweet. Fast reads (O(1)), expensive writes for celebrities.
Fan-out on read (pull): read all followed users' latest tweets at query time. O(following count) reads per timeline view. Slow reads, no write overhead.
Hybrid: fan-out on write for normal users, fan-out on read for celebrities (>1M followers). Twitter uses this exact approach.
Timeline cache: Redis sorted set (ZSET) with tweet ID as score. ZRANGE to get latest N tweets. Evict old entries to bound memory per user.
sessions.content:
## Twitter Timeline — A Complete Scalability Case Study ### The Scale - 350 million daily active users - 500 million tweets/day (~6,000 tweets/second average, 100,000+ at peak) - Average user follows 200 accounts - Celebrity users: 50–100 million followers - Timeline load: 1 view = fetch ~100 recent tweets from followed accounts ### Approach 1: Fan-Out on Read (Pull Model) ```python class PullTimeline: def get_timeline(self, user_id: str, limit: int = 100) -> list: following = db.get_following(user_id) # e.g. 200 users tweets = [] for followed_id in following: user_tweets = db.get_latest_tweets(followed_id, n=limit) tweets.extend(user_tweets) tweets.sort(key=lambda t: t["created_at"], reverse=True) return tweets[:limit] # Problem: # 350M users × 200 following × 100 tweets = 7 trillion DB reads/day # p99 latency: seconds (N sequential DB queries or N parallel but high fanout) ``` ### Approach 2: Fan-Out on Write (Push Model) ```python import redis import json redis_client = redis.Redis(host="redis", decode_responses=True) class PushTimeline: MAX_TIMELINE_SIZE = 800 # keep last 800 tweets per user def on_tweet_created(self, tweet: dict): author_id = tweet["user_id"] tweet_id = tweet["id"] followers = db.get_followers(author_id) # could be 50M! # Write tweet to every follower's timeline cache # Twitter does this with a distributed task queue for follower_id in followers: timeline_key = f"timeline:{follower_id}" # Redis ZADD: sorted set, score = timestamp for ordering redis_client.zadd(timeline_key, {json.dumps(tweet): tweet["created_at"]}) # Trim to last 800 tweets (evict oldest) redis_client.zremrangebyrank(timeline_key, 0, -(self.MAX_TIMELINE_SIZE + 1)) def get_timeline(self, user_id: str, limit: int = 100) -> list: timeline_key = f"timeline:{user_id}" # O(log N + limit) read — fast! raw_tweets = redis_client.zrevrange(timeline_key, 0, limit - 1) return [json.loads(t) for t in raw_tweets] # Problem: # Celebrity tweet -> 50M write operations -> takes minutes even with worker fleet # Memory: 350M users × 800 tweets × 500 bytes = 140 TB Redis memory ``` ### Approach 3: Hybrid (Twitter's Actual Approach) ```mermaid graph TD Tweet["New Tweet Created"] --> Check{"Author follower count"} Check -->|"< 1M followers"| FanOut["Fan-out on write
(push to follower timelines)"] Check -->|"> 1M followers"| Flag["Mark as celebrity tweet
(no fan-out)"] FanOut --> Cache["Redis Timeline Cache
(per user, ZSET)"] ReadReq["Timeline Read Request"] --> ReadCache["Read user's cached timeline"] ReadCache --> Merge["Merge with celebrity tweets
(fetch at read time for followed celebs)"] Merge --> Response["Sorted timeline response"] style Flag fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff style Merge fill:#e67e22,color:#fff ``` ```typescript // Hybrid timeline read async function getTimeline(userId: string, limit: number = 100): Promise { // 1. Get pre-computed timeline from Redis (fast) const cachedTweets = await redis.zrevrange(`timeline:${userId}`, 0, limit - 1); // 2. Get celebrity accounts this user follows const following = await db.getFollowing(userId); const celebs = following.filter(u => u.followerCount > 1_000_000); // 3. Fetch recent tweets from celebrities at read time const celebTweets = await Promise.all( celebs.map(c => redis.zrevrange(`tweets:${c.id}`, 0, 9)) // last 10 per celeb ); // 4. Merge and sort const allTweets = [ ...cachedTweets.map(t => JSON.parse(t)), ...celebTweets.flat().map(t => JSON.parse(t)), ]; return allTweets .sort((a, b) => b.createdAt - a.createdAt) .slice(0, limit); } ``` ### Distributed Fan-Out Infrastructure ```python from celery import Celery app = Celery("timeline", broker="redis://redis:6379/0") @app.task(rate_limit="50000/s") # limit fan-out worker throughput def push_to_follower_timeline(follower_id: str, tweet: dict): timeline_key = f"timeline:{follower_id}" redis_client.zadd(timeline_key, {json.dumps(tweet): tweet["created_at"]}) redis_client.zremrangebyrank(timeline_key, 0, -801) def fan_out_tweet(tweet: dict, follower_ids: list): # Dispatch individual tasks — parallelised across worker fleet # Twitter uses ~100 fan-out workers per region for follower_id in follower_ids: push_to_follower_timeline.delay(follower_id, tweet) print(f"Dispatched {len(follower_ids)} fan-out tasks for tweet {tweet['id']}") # Performance: 100 workers × 500 writes/sec each = 50,000 writes/sec # Normal user (1,000 followers): 1,000 / 50,000 = 0.02 seconds to fan out # Large user (1M followers): 1M / 50,000 = 20 seconds — still fast enough ``` ### Memory Calculation ```python def timeline_memory_estimate( users: int = 350_000_000, tweets_per_timeline: int = 800, bytes_per_tweet_ref: int = 50, # store tweet ID + metadata, not full content active_user_fraction: float = 0.20, # only cache active users ) -> dict: active_users = int(users * active_user_fraction) total_bytes = active_users * tweets_per_timeline * bytes_per_tweet_ref total_gb = total_bytes / 1e9 return { "active_users_cached": f"{active_users:,}", "total_timeline_cache_gb": round(total_gb, 0), "redis_nodes_needed_128gb": round(total_gb / 128), } print(timeline_memory_estimate()) # active_users_cached: 70,000,000 # total_timeline_cache_gb: 2800.0 GB # redis_nodes_needed_128gb: 22 nodes ``` ## Interview Q&A **Q: How do you handle a tweet deletion in the push model?** A: Soft delete: mark tweet as deleted in DB. Keep tweet IDs in timelines. At read time, filter out deleted tweet IDs. Eventually: background job removes deleted IDs from timeline caches. This avoids the expensive operation of finding all timelines containing a tweet. **Q: How does Twitter handle timeline consistency?** A: It does not. Timelines are eventually consistent. During fan-out, different followers see the tweet at different times (within seconds for most). This is an AP choice — availability and speed are prioritised over immediate global consistency. **Q: What is the n+1 problem in timeline generation?** A: When rendering a timeline of N tweets from N different users, naively making N separate user-profile requests is the n+1 problem. Fix: batch the user IDs into a single multi-get request (Redis MGET, SQL WHERE id IN (...)). Twitter stores tweet author metadata inline in the tweet record to avoid this.

quizBank.question: What is the primary advantage of horizontal scaling over vertical scaling?
options: B) It eliminates the single point of failure and has no theoretical upper limit
C) It requires no changes to application code
quizBank.explanation: Horizontal scaling adds more servers, eliminating single points of failure and enabling theoretically unlimited scale. Vertical scaling has hard limits (max instance size) and maintains SPOF.

quizBank.question: Which of the following makes a service stateless?
options: A) Storing sessions in server memory
B) Using JWT tokens and Redis for session storage
C) Using sticky sessions in the load balancer
D) Storing user data in a local file
quizBank.explanation: Stateless: no session state on the server. JWT encodes session in token (client-side). Redis stores shared session state accessible by any server instance. Any server can handle any request.

quizBank.question: Back-pressure allows a consumer to signal a producer to slow its output rate.
quizBank.explanation: Back-pressure is a flow control mechanism where a downstream consumer signals upstream producers to slow down when overwhelmed. TCP uses back-pressure via receive window size. Reactive Streams specification standardizes back-pressure for async streams.

quizBank.question: Explain the bulkhead pattern with a real-world analogy.
quizBank.explanation: Real-world analogy: ship compartments (bulkheads). If one compartment floods, watertight doors prevent flooding the entire ship. In software: separate resource pools (thread pools, connection pools) for different dependencies. If payment service becomes slow and exhausts its thread pool, the inventory thread pool is unaffected. Inventory service continues functioning. Without bulkheads: shared thread pool exhausted by slow payment service → entire application unresponsive. With bulkheads: blast radius limited to the failing component.

quizBank.question: An application server uses in-memory sessions. It has 5 instances behind a load balancer. What problem occurs and how do you fix it?
quizBank.explanation: Problem: user logs in, session created on Server 1. Next request goes to Server 2 (round-robin) → no session → user is logged out. Solutions: (1) Sticky sessions (IP hash or cookie): always route user to same server. Problem: breaks if server dies. Defeats load balancing. (2) Shared session store (Redis): sessions stored in Redis, any server can access. Best solution for stateless scaling. (3) JWT tokens: session encoded in signed token, no server-side storage. Stateless by design. Recommendation: Redis-backed sessions or JWT. Never rely on in-memory sessions for horizontally scaled services.

quizBank.question: Design a system that scales from 100 users to 1 million users over 12 months.
quizBank.explanation: Phase 1 (100 users): Single server. Single DB. Simple is OK. Phase 2 (10k users): Add CDN for static assets. Add read replica for DB reads. Connection pool. Redis for sessions. Phase 3 (100k users): Horizontal scaling with Auto Scaling Group (2-10 instances). Redis cluster for cache. Separate DB server. Background job queue (SQS). Phase 4 (1M users): CDN for API responses (where possible). Geographic expansion (2 regions). DB sharding or switch to cloud-managed DB (Aurora). Dedicated microservices for hot paths. Kafka for event streaming. Phase 5 (10M users): Full microservices. Multiple DB shards. Multi-region. Dedicated infrastructure teams. Key principle: don't build for 1M users when you have 100. Add complexity exactly when needed.

quizBank.question: What is the difference between performance and scalability?
quizBank.explanation: Performance: how fast the system serves a single request. Latency of one request. Can be poor for one user even when no load. Scalability: how system behavior changes as load increases. A system is scalable if performance degrades gracefully with load. Contrasts: Fast but not scalable: responds in 10ms with 1 user. Responds in 10s with 1000 users. Scalable but slow: responds in 500ms with 1 user. Still 500ms with 10000 users (linear scaling). Ideal: fast AND scalable. Fix performance first: optimizing a query from 100ms to 10ms means you need 10x less hardware. Then ensure the fast system also scales. Amdahl's Law: max speedup from parallelism limited by sequential fraction.

quizBank.question: How does queue-based load leveling prevent system overload?
quizBank.explanation: Load leveling: queue absorbs burst traffic, workers process at sustainable rate. Without queue: 10k RPS spike hits DB directly → DB overloaded → timeouts → cascading failure. With queue: 10k RPS → queue (can hold millions of messages). Workers consume at 1k RPS → DB at steady state load. Cost: latency. Requests don't complete immediately, they complete when processed (minutes later for burst). Acceptable for: email sending, report generation, image processing, order fulfillment. Not acceptable for: real-time queries, payment confirmation (users expect immediate result). For payment: queue for background processing (send receipt, update analytics) but give immediate sync response.

quizBank.question: What is Amdahl's Law and how does it limit horizontal scaling?
quizBank.explanation: Amdahl's Law: maximum speedup from N processors = 1 / (S + (1-S)/N) where S = sequential fraction. Example: if 10% of work is sequential, max speedup with infinite processors = 10x. With 100 processors: 1/(0.1 + 0.9/100) = ~9.2x speedup. Implications for scaling: if your application has serialized bottlenecks (DB writes, sequential business logic), adding servers gives diminishing returns. You need to identify and parallelize or eliminate sequential sections. Practical example: lock contention in DB → horizontally scaling app servers doesn't help because all hit the same lock. Fix the sequential bottleneck (lock) before adding app servers.

quizBank.question: What are the 12-Factor App principles and why do they enable scalability?
quizBank.explanation: Key principles for scalability: (1) Processes — stateless, share-nothing. Each process independent → can scale horizontally without coordination. (2) Config — in environment variables. Different configs per environment without code changes → easy to scale across environments. (3) Backing services — databases, caches as attached resources. Swap backing service without code change → switch from single Redis to Redis Cluster transparently. (4) Port binding — self-contained. No dependency on runtime (Tomcat). Any instance serves its own port. (5) Concurrency — scale via process model. Multiple stateless processes behind LB. (6) Disposability — fast startup, graceful shutdown. Auto-scaling requires instances to start and stop quickly. (7) Logs — event streams. Centralized logging works with N instances.

quizBank.question: Design an auto-scaling strategy for a video transcoding service with highly variable load.
quizBank.explanation: Video transcoding: CPU-intensive, long-running jobs (minutes to hours). Strategy: (1) Queue-based: jobs queued in SQS. Workers pull and process. (2) Scale metric: SQS queue depth (not CPU - CPU will be 100% on active workers). Auto-scale out when queue depth > 10 messages. Scale in when queue is empty. (3) Worker instances: GPU instances (g4dn) for hardware-accelerated transcoding. Spot instances for cost (GPU spot can be 70% cheaper). Mixed: on-demand for base capacity, spot for burst. (4) Scale-in protection: instances with in-flight jobs protected from termination. SQS visibility timeout = expected job duration. (5) Warm-up: none needed (workers immediately start polling). (6) Cost optimization: scale to 0 during quiet periods. Use SQS lifecycle hook to drain jobs before termination.

quizBank.question: What is sticky session and why is it considered an anti-pattern?
options: A) A session that lasts longer than usual
B) Routing same client to same server, making the application stateful
C) A session encrypted with a sticky key
D) A session stored in a database
quizBank.explanation: Sticky sessions route a client to the same server based on cookie or IP hash. Anti-pattern because: (1) Defeats load balancing — uneven distribution. (2) If sticky server dies, all its users lose sessions. (3) Prevents graceful shutdown of servers. (4) Complicates auto-scaling. Fix: use shared session store (Redis) instead.

quizBank.question: How do you implement graceful shutdown to support auto-scaling?
quizBank.explanation: Graceful shutdown: when instance is terminating, complete in-flight requests before exiting. Without it: auto-scale in kills servers mid-request → errors for users. Implementation: (1) Register SIGTERM handler. (2) On SIGTERM: stop accepting new connections (remove from LB). (3) Wait for in-flight requests to complete (max 30s). (4) Close DB connections. (5) Exit. Kubernetes: preStop hook runs before SIGTERM. terminationGracePeriodSeconds = max request duration. AWS: Auto Scaling lifecycle hook → instance enters Terminating:Wait → LB deregisters → wait for connection draining → continue termination. Load balancer connection draining: ALB waits up to 300s for in-flight requests to complete before removing instance.

quizBank.question: A service makes calls to 3 external APIs. One API becomes slow. How does bulkhead help?
quizBank.explanation: Without bulkhead: all 3 APIs share one thread pool (20 threads). Slow API takes 30s per call → 20 threads blocked → thread pool exhausted → calls to OTHER 2 APIs also blocked (starvation) → entire service unresponsive. With bulkhead: 3 separate pools: API1Pool(10), API2Pool(10), API3Pool(5). Slow API exhausts API1Pool. API2Pool and API3Pool unaffected. Service partially functional. Degraded but not down. Implementation: Java Executors.newFixedThreadPool per API. Hystrix/Resilience4j BulkheadConfig. Combined with circuit breaker: once API1Pool is full, circuit opens — fail fast without blocking threads.

quizBank.question: How do you scale a WebSocket service horizontally?
quizBank.explanation: WebSocket challenge: stateful persistent connections. Client connects to Server 1. Server 1 fails → connection lost. Server 2 knows nothing about client. Solutions: (1) Pub/sub routing: each server subscribes to Redis pub/sub channel for its connected clients. Message for client A → published to Redis → server holding A's connection delivers it. (2) Sticky sessions at LB: route client to same server via cookie. Problem: server failure = connection loss. (3) Websocket gateway: dedicated stateful gateway (e.g., Centrifugo, Pusher) that handles connections. App servers are stateless. (4) Client reconnection: clients automatically reconnect when connection drops. Use exponential backoff. Reconnect to any server. State sync after reconnect. Best: pub/sub routing (Redis) + client auto-reconnect. Scales horizontally while handling server failures gracefully.

quizBank.question: Explain the X, Y, Z axes of the Scale Cube.
quizBank.explanation: Scale Cube (Abbott & Fisher): three dimensions of scaling. X-axis (Clone): run multiple copies of the application behind a load balancer. Identical servers, distribute load. Requires stateless design. Simple first step. Y-axis (Decompose): split by function (microservices). Order service, User service, Payment service each scale independently. Enables different scaling strategies per service. Z-axis (Shard): partition data. Each instance handles a subset of data (shard by user ID, geography). Scales data volume beyond single node. Use in combination: X first (easy). Y when different services have different scaling needs. Z when data volume exceeds single node capacity.

quizBank.question: What is Amdahl's Law parallel limit for a system where 5% of work must be sequential?
quizBank.explanation: Amdahl's Law: max speedup = 1/S where S is sequential fraction. S=0.05 (5%): max speedup = 1/0.05 = 20x. No matter how many processors you add, sequential 5% limits total speedup to 20x. This is why eliminating bottlenecks is so valuable: reducing S from 5% to 1% increases max speedup from 20x to 100x.

quizBank.question: Design a system to handle Black Friday traffic (100x normal load) for an e-commerce site.
quizBank.explanation: Pre-event preparation: (1) Load testing: simulate 150x load to validate capacity. (2) Pre-scaling: scale out 1 week before (don't wait for auto-scale). (3) CDN pre-warming: push product catalog and images to CDN edges globally. (4) DB read replicas: add 3 additional replicas. During event: (1) Static content: CDN for all product pages, images (95% of traffic). (2) Dynamic: auto-scaling group for app servers (min 20, max 200). (3) Checkout flow: queue-backed. Order placed → SQS → order processor at 10k/min rate. Immediate 'order received' response to user. (4) Inventory: distributed counter in Redis. Reserve in Redis first, sync to DB async. (5) Rate limiting: 1000 RPS per user to prevent bots. (6) Graceful degradation: disable non-critical features (reviews, recommendations). (7) Circuit breakers: payment gateway calls with 5s timeout + retry. Monitoring: real-time dashboard with 30s refresh. PagerDuty alert if error rate > 1%.

quizBank.question: True or False: Vertical scaling eliminates the need for stateless service design.
quizBank.explanation: Vertical scaling (bigger server) still leaves you with a single server — if it fails, service is down. Stateless design is required for high availability (multiple servers) regardless of whether you also vertically scale. Furthermore, vertical scaling has hard limits, so stateless design is eventually required for scaling.

interviewTips: Always start by clarifying functional and non-functional requirements
Never jump to a solution — walk through your thought process step by step
Mention trade-offs explicitly: consistency vs availability, latency vs throughput
Draw diagrams! Use boxes for services, cylinders for databases, arrows for data flow
Start with a simple design, then scale it — don't over-engineer from the start
Always discuss failure modes: what happens when X goes down?
Mention real-world systems: 'Netflix uses X for this' shows depth
Back-of-envelope calculations impress interviewers — estimate QPS, storage, bandwidth
Discuss monitoring and observability — how would you know if something is wrong?
End with a summary of key decisions and their trade-offs
commonMistakes: Not understanding the fundamentals of Scalability Patterns
Jumping to implementation without clarifying requirements
Ignoring edge cases and error handling
Not discussing trade-offs between different approaches
Memorizing solutions instead of understanding patterns

cheatSheet:
# Content Delivery Networks (CDN)

## Definition
A CDN is a geographically distributed network of servers (edge nodes/PoPs) that cache and serve content close to users. Reduces latency by serving from nearby location instead of distant origin server.

## How CDN Works

1. User requests `https://example.com/image.jpg`
2. DNS resolves to nearest CDN edge node (via Anycast or GeoDNS)
3. Edge checks local cache:
   - **Cache HIT**: returns content directly (< 10ms)
   - **Cache MISS**: fetches from origin, caches, returns (100-500ms)
4. Subsequent requests from same region → cache HIT

```mermaid
graph LR
    User([User\nNew York]) --> Edge1[CDN Edge\nNYC]
    User2([User\nLondon]) --> Edge2[CDN Edge\nLondon]
    User3([User\nTokyo]) --> Edge3[CDN Edge\nTokyo]
    Edge1 -->|Cache miss| Shield[Origin Shield\nUS-East]
    Edge2 -->|Cache miss| Shield
    Edge3 -->|Cache miss| Origin[Origin Server]
    Shield -->|Cache miss| Origin
```

## Edge Caching

### Cache-Control Headers (from Origin)
```http
# Static assets: cache 1 year, immutable (content-addressed filenames)
Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable

# API responses: cache 5 min at CDN, 1 min in browser
Cache-Control: public, s-maxage=300, max-age=60

# Vary: don't serve cached response to wrong language
Vary: Accept-Language, Accept-Encoding

# ETag for conditional requests
ETag: "686897696a7c876b7e"
```

### Conditional Requests
```
Client → If-None-Match: "686897696a7c876b7e" → CDN
CDN → 304 Not Modified (if unchanged, no body → saves bandwidth)
```

## Origin Shield

- Intermediate caching layer between edge nodes and origin
- Prevents many edge nodes all hitting origin on cache miss
- One shield per region → shields all regional edges
- Dramatically reduces origin load

```
Without shield: 200 edge nodes × cache miss = 200 origin requests
With shield:    200 edge nodes → 1 shield → 1 origin request
```

## Push vs Pull CDN

### Pull CDN (Most Common)
- Content served from origin on first request (cache miss)
- CDN pulls from origin on demand
- Easy setup — just point CDN to origin
- Good for: large content libraries, infrequently accessed content

### Push CDN
- Pre-load content to CDN before any user requests
- Upload content manually or via API
- Good for: known content (software releases, video files), ensuring cache is warm
- Storage cost: pay for storage on all PoPs

## CDN Cache Invalidation

### URL-Based Purge
```bash
# CloudFront invalidation
aws cloudfront create-invalidation \
  --distribution-id ABCDEF123456 \
  --paths '/images/logo.png' '/css/main.css'
```
- Expensive if many URLs
- Propagation delay: seconds to minutes

### Content Versioning (Preferred)
```html
<!-- Old: must invalidate CDN when changed -->
<link href="/css/main.css">

<!-- New: new filename = new cache, no invalidation needed -->
<link href="/css/main.a7c3d2f1.css">
```
- Hash of content in filename
- Never need to invalidate CDN (old files auto-expire)
- Requires build system integration (Webpack, Vite)

### Surrogate Keys / Cache Tags
```
Surrogate-Key: product-123 category-electronics
```
- Tag cached objects with logical keys
- Purge all objects with tag `product-123` in one API call
- Fastly and Cloudflare support this natively

## Major CDN Providers

### CloudFront (AWS)
- Tightly integrated with AWS (S3, ALB, Lambda@Edge)
- Lambda@Edge: run custom code at edge nodes
- Global: 450+ PoPs
- Price: $0.0085/GB in US

### Cloudflare
- 300+ PoPs, largest network
- Workers: serverless functions at edge
- DDoS protection built-in
- Free tier available
- DNS-based routing via Anycast

### Akamai
- Oldest, most enterprise
- 4000+ PoPs
- Best for complex enterprise requirements
- EdgeSuite for dynamic content acceleration

### Fastly
- Real-time purging (< 1 second)
- VCL (Varnish Configuration Language) for custom logic
- Surrogate key support
- Favorite for API caching

## Dynamic Content Acceleration

CDN is not just for static content:
- **TCP optimization**: persistent connections to origin, TCP Fast Open
- **TLS termination at edge**: TLS handshake near user (latency reduced)
- **HTTP/2 multiplexing**: single connection for many requests
- **Compression**: gzip/brotli at edge
- **Edge computing**: Cloudflare Workers, Lambda@Edge for personalization

## Security Features

- **DDoS protection**: absorbs volumetric attacks at edge
- **WAF (Web Application Firewall)**: filter malicious requests at edge
- **TLS/SSL**: terminate HTTPS at edge, HTTP to origin (or mTLS)
- **Rate limiting**: per-IP rate limiting at edge
- **Bot protection**: challenge suspicious clients

## CDN Architecture for System Design

```mermaid
graph TB
    User --> Anycast[Anycast DNS]
    Anycast --> POP1[PoP - New York]
    Anycast --> POP2[PoP - London]
    Anycast --> POP3[PoP - Tokyo]
    
    POP1 --> Shield1[Origin Shield\nUS-East]
    POP2 --> Shield2[Origin Shield\nEurope]
    POP3 --> Shield3[Origin Shield\nAPAC]
    
    Shield1 & Shield2 & Shield3 --> Origin[Origin Servers\nLoad Balanced]
    Origin --> S3[S3 / Object Store]
```

## Pros and Cons

### Pros
- Dramatically reduced latency for global users
- Offloads origin server (handle 10-100x more traffic)
- DDoS protection at network edge
- Improved availability (edge serves cache during origin outages)
- Cost reduction (CDN bandwidth < origin bandwidth)

### Cons
- Cost (bandwidth charges at scale)
- Cache invalidation complexity
- Debugging: hard to see what's cached where
- Stale content risks
- Not suitable for highly dynamic/personalized content

## Real-World Examples
- **Netflix**: Open Connect CDN (custom-built), serves 200M+ subscribers, 2TB+ of video per second
- **Cloudflare**: Handles 10% of all internet traffic
- **Amazon**: CloudFront serves AWS console, S3 assets, Prime Video
- **GitHub**: Fastly for code download and static assets

## Common FAANG Interview Questions
1. When would you use a CDN? When would you not?
2. How do CDNs reduce latency compared to serving from origin?
3. How do you handle cache invalidation for API responses in a CDN?
4. What is origin shield and why is it important?
5. How does Anycast routing work?

plan.overview: CDN is the highest-leverage optimization for global applications. The mental model: instead of having all users travel to your data center, bring your data center to each user. Master Cache-Control headers and invalidation strategies — these are what determine CDN effectiveness.
plan.skippedTopics: We skip CDN billing optimization (provider-specific). We skip ISP-level caching. We skip QUIC/HTTP3 CDN acceleration (covered in Networking). We focus on the CDN design patterns used in interviews.

sessions.content:
## What is a CDN?

A CDN (Content Delivery Network) caches your content at edge servers worldwide, reducing latency by serving data from the nearest location.

### How It Works
```mermaid
graph LR
    User -->|Request| Edge[CDN Edge Server]
    Edge -->|Cache Hit| User
    Edge -->|Cache Miss| Origin[Origin Server]
    Origin --> Edge
    Edge --> User
```

### Key Concepts
- **Edge Server**: Server close to users (100+ locations globally)
- **Origin Server**: Your actual server
- **Cache Hit Ratio**: % of requests served from edge (target: >95%)
- **TTL (Time To Live)**: How long content stays cached

### Cache-Control Headers
```
Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000  // Static assets (1 year)
Cache-Control: public, max-age=60, stale-while-revalidate=30  // API
Cache-Control: no-store  // Sensitive data (never cache)
```

### CDN Providers
| Provider | Best For | Free Tier |
|----------|---------|----------|
| CloudFront | AWS integration | 1TB/month |
| Cloudflare | DDoS + performance | Generous free |
| Akamai | Enterprise scale | No |
| Fastly | Real-time purging | Limited |

### Push vs Pull CDN
- **Pull**: CDN fetches from origin on first request (most common)
- **Push**: You upload content to CDN proactively (for large files)

### Cache Invalidation
```bash
# Purge specific URL
aws cloudfront create-invalidation --paths '/api/products/*'

# Better: Use versioned URLs
# app.a1b2c3.js (hash in filename = infinite cache + instant updates)
```

### Interview Tip
"For static assets, I'd use a CDN with content-hash filenames and max-age=1year. For dynamic API responses, CDN with short TTL (60s) and stale-while-revalidate for zero-latency refreshes."
